#### PHX200 / PHX600 (Johnson Controls FHI Board) — N2 ADDRESS SETTING
The PHX N2 address is set via **software** using HVAC PRO for Windows (Rev 4.0+), not via hardware jumpers.
- Each PHX on the N2 Bus must have a unique address.
- Maximum N2 bus devices: 32 per bus segment (standard Metasys N2 Bus rules).
- N2 Bus end-of-line termination required at both physical ends.
- If replacing PHX board: download existing database from HVAC PRO before removal. Re-download to new board after installation.

**EXP Board (Expander) diagnostic LEDs:**
- LEDs on EXP boards indicate communication status between EXP and FHI.
- If EXP LED is off or blinking abnormally, check 40-pin ribbon cable connections between MIJ → EXP → FHI.
- PHX200 has one EXP; PHX600 has two EXPs.

---

#### PNEUMATIC VALVE CONTROLLERS — ANALOG / LEGACY
For pneumatic actuated Phoenix Controls valves (Control Type = B in model string):

**Pivot Arm Alignment** (CV and Two-State pneumatic systems):
- Incorrect pivot arm alignment is the most common cause of wrong flow on a pneumatic CV or two-state valve.
- CV system wrong flow: realign the pivot arm to correct valve position.
- Two-state system wrong position: check pneumatic tubing and pressure FIRST, then realign pivot arm.
- Pivot arm hole sizes differ by vintage: Vintage A/B/C = 3/16"; Vintage D/E = 1/4".

**Pressure Switch (PSL option):**
- When replacing legacy pneumatic valve controller: note pressure switch and hose orientation BEFORE removal. New pressure switch orientation MUST replicate original exactly.
- Blocked or kinked pressure switch tubing = flow alarm even when fan is running.
- If pressure switch tubing is blocked: straighten/replace tubing, verify static pressure across valve (need min 0.3" WC LP, 0.5" WC MP).

**Air Supply Requirements for pneumatic valves:**
- Clean, dry instrument air required. Moisture in pneumatic lines = sluggish response, sticking actuator, tubing corrosion.
- Typical supply pressure: 15–20 PSI at controller.
- Max pneumatic tubing run to controller: 75 ft (22.8m). Beyond this = sluggish or no response.
- I/P transducer signal range: 4–20mA input → 3–15 PSI pneumatic output.
- Fail positions: normally closed (air-to-open), normally open (air-to-close) — verify which type before working on system.

**Analog Pneumatic Vintage identification (for upgrade/replacement work):**
- Vintage A/B: U-channel base, Accel I hourglass shape, Telco PCB connectors, 3/16" pivot arm holes, two actuators per dual valve.
- Vintage C: Flat base, Accel II diffused shape, Telco PCB, 3/16" pivot.
- Vintage D/E: Flat base, Accel II, terminal block PCB, 1/4" pivot arm holes, one actuator per dual.

---

#### BOARD REPLACEMENT CHECKLIST — USE EVERY TIME
(1) Document: photograph wiring, record all calibration values. (2) Power down completely. (3) Discharge capacitors (Celeris FSM: J1 discharge procedure). (4) Verify new board power supply type (±15VDC vs 24VAC) and hardware config before installing. (5) Install and rewire. (6) Power up — check indicators (Er_c expected on new FHM). (7) **Set Parameter 1 / mode FIRST** before any other calibration. (8) Complete full calibration from Room Schedule Sheet. (9) Test all functions: sash, alarms, ZPS setback, emergency exhaust. (10) Label: date, tech, final values.

## WHY PIN AND JUMPER SETTINGS MATTER ON BOARD SWAP
This is one of the most common causes of a new board that powers up but does NOT function correctly after installation. A replacement board ships from the factory in a DEFAULT configuration that may not match your application. If you install it without verifying and setting pins/jumpers to match the original board, the board will malfunction — often in ways that look like a calibration failure or hardware fault, masking the real cause.

**The cardinal rule**: Before powering up any replacement board, physically verify every jumper and DIP switch matches the original board configuration. Do this BEFORE calibration. Do this BEFORE connecting to the network. Photograph the original board's jumper/switch positions before removal.

---

## FHM430 / FHM530 / FHM631 (X30 SERIES) — PIN/JUMPER REFERENCE

topic: FHM Pin/Jumper Complete Reference — TB4, J1–J4, Parameter 1
content:
## FHM430 / FHM530 / FHM631 (X30 SERIES) — PIN/JUMPER REFERENCE

### TB4 Power Supply Selection — MUST VERIFY BEFORE INSTALLING
The X30 FHM boards support two power input types. Installing a board configured for the wrong supply type will damage it instantly or cause erratic behavior.

| TB4 Pin | ±15VDC System | 24VAC System |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Pin 1 | +15VDC | 24VAC Hot (H) |
| Pin 2 | Functional Ground | Functional Ground |
| Pin 3 | -15VDC | 24VAC Neutral (N) |

**How to verify before installing**: Measure TB4 Pin 1 to Pin 2 on the existing installation BEFORE removing the old board. If you read +15VDC → ±15VDC system. If you read ~24VAC → 24VAC system. The replacement board must match or it will fail immediately.

### J-Series Jumpers on FHM Boards
**J1 — External Emergency Input polarity:**
| J1 Position | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| N.O. | Emergency input triggers on contact CLOSE (active low) — most common |
| N.C. | Emergency input triggers on contact OPEN (active high) |
- Default from factory: N.O. (normally open trigger)
- **If wrong**: emergency exhaust will either trigger constantly or never trigger

**J2 — Sash sensor signal type (FHM430/FHM631 only):**
| J2 Position | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| V | Voltage input sash sensor (0–10VDC) |
| R | Resistance input sash sensor (potentiometer) |
- Default: V (voltage)
- **If wrong**: sash position will read incorrectly at all positions — board will appear to commission but sash tracking will be wrong, causing incorrect CFM commands and face velocity alarms

**J3 — Alarm relay type (FHM530 only):**
| J3 Position | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| N.O. | Alarm relay normally open (closes on alarm) |
| N.C. | Alarm relay normally closed (opens on alarm) |
- Match to downstream BMS wiring. If wrong: alarm signal to BMS will be inverted — system shows alarm when normal, normal when alarming

**J4 — ZPS input type:**
| J4 Position | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| DC | ZPS100 series (DC powered) |
| AC | ZPS200 series (AC powered) |
- **If wrong**: ZPS will not trigger setback correctly, or may cause false setback / false occupied readings

**Parameter 1 (software "pin") — FHM Mode — MUST SET BEFORE ANY OTHER PARAMETER:**
This is the software equivalent of a mode jumper. It tells the board what type of valve/application it is controlling. Shipping default is often vAvd (VAV Drive) which is NOT the most common application.

| Parameter 1 Value | Application |
|-------------------|-------------|
| vAvv | Standard VAV (most fume hoods) — EXV with sash sensor |
| vAvd | VAV Drive — variable speed drive application |
| cvn | Constant Volume — no sash sensor |
| cvA | Constant Volume — sash alarm only |
| cvS | Constant Volume — ZPS setback |
| cvSA | Constant Volume — ZPS setback + sash alarm |
| 2St | Two-State — FHM530 only |
| 2StS | Two-State with ZPS setback |
| 2StA | Two-State with sash alarm |

**Consequence of wrong Parameter 1**: If board is in vAvv (VAV) but application is cvn (CV no sash), the board will immediately throw a flow alarm and sash sensor fault because it expects a sash signal that isn't wired. Calibration will appear to complete but the system will not function. Similarly, if board is in cvn but application is vAvv, the valve will not respond to sash position changes — the board ignores the sash sensor entirely.

---

## CELERIS LVC / ADV / CCU — PIN/JUMPER REFERENCE

### J1 — Fail-Safe Position (MOST CRITICAL JUMPER)
| J1 | Fail-Safe Behavior |
|----|-------------------|
| N.O. | Valve fails OPEN on power loss |
| N.C. | Valve fails CLOSED on power loss |
| No jumper | Valve holds LAST POSITION on power loss |

**Consequence of wrong J1**: A supply valve that should fail closed (J1=N.C.) but is set to fail open (J1=N.O.) will flood the space with uncontrolled supply air on any power interruption. An exhaust valve that should fail open but is set to N.C. will close on power loss, trapping contaminants. In BSL/fume hood applications, wrong fail-safe = immediate safety violation.

**Procedure to verify**: Check the original board's J1 position BEFORE removal. Also check the Room Schedule Sheet (RSS) — the design engineer specifies the required fail-safe position for every valve.

### J2 — Network Address Source
| J2 | Node Address Source |
|----|-------------------|
| SW | Address from DIP switch SW1 (manual addressing) |
| NET | Address from LonWorks network binding (automatic) |
- Most Celeris 2 installations use NET (network-assigned). Celeris 1 typically uses SW.
- **If wrong**: board will not respond to network commands (NET mode) or will conflict with network addressing (SW mode with duplicate address)

### DIP Switch SW1 — MS/TP MAC Address / LonWorks Node Address
On boards that use hardware addressing (J2=SW):
- SW1 positions 1–7 set the binary node address (0–127)
- **Must match the original board's SW1 exactly**
- Duplicate address on trunk = two devices conflict, both intermittently drop offline
- Verify the address in Phoenix Controls Workbench or Accel-Works BEFORE removing the original board

### J3 — Baud Rate Selection (some Celeris 1 LVC models)
| J3 | Baud Rate |
|----|-----------|
| 1 | 9600 bps |
| 2 | 19200 bps |
| 3 | 38400 bps |
| 4 | 76800 bps |
- Must match all other devices on the trunk
- **If wrong**: board appears powered but invisible to network — no communication at all

### J4 — Actuator Type
| J4 | Actuator |
|----|----------|
| HS | High-speed electric actuator (CSCP ACM-compatible) |
| SS | Standard-speed electric actuator |
| PN | Pneumatic actuator (I/P transducer output) |
- **If wrong**: actuator will receive incorrect signal type — high-speed actuator getting standard-speed signal moves sluggishly or not at all; pneumatic actuator getting electric signal does nothing

---

## FHI100-0 (JOHNSON CONTROLS) — DIP SWITCH REFERENCE

### SW1/SW2 — Analog Input Configuration (CRITICAL — 6 inputs, each has 2 switches)
Every analog input (AI1–AI6) has two DIP switches that MUST match the sensor/signal type wired to that input. Installing with wrong switch positions = wrong readings on all affected inputs = wrong temperature/humidity/flow data fed to the system.

| SW1/x | SW2/x | Input Type | Impedance |
|-------|-------|------------|-----------|
| ON | OFF | RTD temperature sensor (1000Ω Ni, Pt, or Si) | 3540Ω |
| OFF | ON | 0–10VDC voltage signal (from MIJ/EXP board) | 99kΩ |
| ON | ON | 0–2VDC voltage signal | 470kΩ |
| OFF | OFF | 2kΩ setpoint potentiometer | — |

**How to verify**: Check the original board's SW1/SW2 positions for each AI before removal. Cross-reference with the FHI wiring diagram — each AI input should have a labeled sensor type.

**Consequence of wrong SW1/SW2**: A temperature RTD input configured as 0–10VDC (SW1=OFF, SW2=ON) will read nonsense — the AI will report a temperature based on electrical resistance as if it were a voltage, giving readings like 400°F or -200°F. A 0–10VDC signal configured as RTD will do the same in reverse. The MIJ thermal demand calculation will be wrong, causing incorrect supply valve commands.

### N2 Bus Address DIP Switches
- Set the unique N2 Bus address for this FHI controller
- Maximum 32 devices per N2 Bus segment
- **Must match the original board's N2 address exactly**
- Set BEFORE downloading database via HVAC PRO
- Duplicate N2 address = communication conflicts across entire PHX system on that bus

---

## MIJ400 / MIJ500 — PIN REFERENCE

The MIJ board has no field-configurable jumpers — it is factory-calibrated and configured by Phoenix Controls. However, it does have terminal block jumpers that must be verified on replacement:

### TB Power Terminal Jumpers
- The MIJ uses ±15VDC power. TB4 carries power.
- TB power connections must be verified to match polarity: Pin 1 = +15VDC, Pin 3 = -15VDC
- **Reversed polarity on TB4 = immediate board damage**. Always verify before powering.

### MIJ Scale Factor Jumpers (MIJ500 only)
The MIJ500 has internal scale factor settings for the high-signal-select thermal demand calculation. These are set at the factory per the application order. If replacing an MIJ500:
- Order the replacement MIJ500 with the same scale factor spec as the original
- Do NOT assume a generic replacement MIJ500 has the correct scale factor
- Incorrect scale factor = wrong thermal demand calculation = supply valve commands wrong CFM for the temperature differential

---

## ACM (ACTUATOR CONTROL MODULE) — DIP/JUMPER REFERENCE

### SW1 — MS/TP MAC Address
The ACM's MAC address on the PBC's MS/TP trunk is set via SW1 DIP switches on the ACM board.
- SW1 positions 1–6 set the binary MAC address (0–63 for ACM devices)
- **Must be set BEFORE installation and BEFORE powering up**
- Each ACM on a PBC trunk must have a unique MAC address
- Duplicate MAC = both ACMs intermittently offline, FHD500 shows Unlinked

**How to verify the correct MAC**: Check the PBC's device list in Phoenix Controls Workbench or Flow Manager App before removing the failed ACM. Note its MAC address. Set SW1 on the replacement ACM to match exactly.

### JP1 — Fail-Safe Module Enable
| JP1 | Function |
|-----|----------|
| Installed | FSM (Fail-Safe Module) enabled — capacitor-based fail-safe active |
| Removed | FSM disabled — valve defaults to spring position on power loss |
- If original ACM had FSM installed, replacement must also have FSM installed AND JP1 set correctly
- **If JP1 removed on FSM-equipped ACM**: capacitor bank will charge but fail-safe won't engage correctly on power loss

### Baud Rate Configuration
ACM baud rate is set in software via Phoenix Controls Workbench (not hardware DIP). However:
- New replacement ACMs ship at factory default baud rate (typically 76800)
- If your trunk runs at a different baud rate, you MUST connect via Workbench and change the baud rate BEFORE adding the ACM to a live trunk
- Adding an ACM at the wrong baud rate to a live trunk causes immediate token-passing conflicts that can disrupt ALL devices on the trunk

---

## UNIVERSAL PRE-INSTALLATION PIN VERIFICATION PROCEDURE

Use this every time before installing any Phoenix Controls replacement board:

**Step 1 — Document the original board (BEFORE power-down):**
□ Photograph every jumper and DIP switch on the original board
□ Record MAC/node address from network tools (Workbench, Flow Manager App, Accel-Works)
□ Record baud rate from network configuration
□ Record power supply type (±15VDC or 24VAC — measure at TB4)
□ Record fail-safe position (check J1 and Room Schedule Sheet)
□ Record Parameter 1 / mode setting (FHM boards — note from display)
□ Record all calibration parameter values (FHM boards — photograph display during parameter scroll)

**Step 2 — Configure replacement board BEFORE installation:**
□ Set all DIP switches to match original board
□ Set all jumpers to match original board
□ Verify power supply type matches installation (±15VDC vs 24VAC)
□ Verify fail-safe jumper matches application requirement (not just original board — cross-check RSS)
□ For ACM: set SW1 MAC address BEFORE connecting to trunk

**Step 3 — Power up and verify before calibration:**
□ Apply power — verify correct indicator behavior (Er_c on FHM = uncommissioned but powered correctly)
□ Verify no smoke, smell, or immediate failure (reversed polarity check)
□ For Celeris LVC: verify correct LED status before binding to network

**Step 4 — Set software mode (FHM boards) BEFORE calibration:**
□ Enter calibration mode
□ Set Parameter 1 to correct application mode FIRST
□ Only after Parameter 1 is correct proceed with remaining 22 parameters

**Step 5 — Calibrate, test, document:**
□ Complete full calibration using RSS values
□ Test all functions including fail-safe (controlled test — know what to expect)
□ Label board with date, tech, and verified pin configuration

---

## QUICK REFERENCE — WRONG PIN SYMPTOMS

| Symptom | Most Likely Pin/Jumper Cause |
|---------|------------------------------|
| New FHM powers up but immediately shows flow alarm | Parameter 1 wrong mode (e.g. vAvv expecting sash that isn't wired) |
| Sash reads wrong at all positions | J2 sash sensor type wrong (V vs R) |
| Emergency exhaust triggers constantly or never | J1 emergency input polarity wrong |
| ZPS setback not working | J4 ZPS type wrong (DC vs AC) |
| Alarm relay inverted at BMS | J3 alarm relay N.O./N.C. wrong |
| New Celeris LVC invisible to network | J2 address source wrong, or J3 baud rate wrong |
| New Celeris valve fails to wrong position on power loss | J1 fail-safe position wrong |
| Two ACMs intermittently drop offline | SW1 duplicate MAC address |
| FHI reads nonsense temperature values | SW1/SW2 analog input type wrong |
| MIJ supply valve commands wrong CFM for temperature | MIJ500 wrong scale factor ordered |
| ACM visible but doesn't communicate after trunk restart | Baud rate mismatch — set in Workbench before adding to live trunk |
| Board powers up but acts like wrong application type | Parameter 1 not set first before calibration |

topic: Niagara 4 Station Architecture and Proxy Points
content:
#### N4 STATION ARCHITECTURE FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS SYSTEMS
The Niagara 4 station database is a hierarchical object model. Everything lives under the station root:
- **Config > Drivers** — all protocol networks (BacnetNetwork, LonNetwork, NiagaraNetwork)
- **Config > Services** — AlarmService, HistoryService, PollSchedulerService, UserService, BackupService
- **Config > Modules** — installed palettes and drivers
- **Config > ProgramService** — custom programming logic
- **Files** — Px graphics, nav files, themes
- **Sys** — system-level properties, modules, platform info

**Phoenix Controls station folder hierarchy (recommended):**
```
Station
└── Config
    └── Drivers
        └── BacnetNetwork
            └── [Building Name]
                └── [Floor/Zone]
                    └── PBC-[Zone Name]  (BACnet device)
                        ├── Zone_Exhaust_CFM  (NumericPoint proxy)
                        ├── Zone_Supply_CFM   (NumericPoint proxy)
                        ├── Room_Offset_CFM   (NumericPoint proxy)
                        ├── Occupied_Cmd      (BooleanWritable proxy)
                        ├── Emergency_Exhaust (BooleanWritable proxy)
                        ├── Flow_Alarm        (BooleanPoint proxy)
                        ├── Pressure_Alarm    (BooleanPoint proxy)
                        └── Hood_1_FV         (NumericPoint proxy)
```

#### STEP-BY-STEP: COMMISSIONING PHOENIX CONTROLS IN N4 WORKBENCH

**Phase 1 — Platform Connection**
1. Open Phoenix Controls Workbench (N4-based, version 3.0+).
2. In Nav tree: Open > Platform > Enter JACE IP address, port 4911, credentials (admin/admin default — change immediately).
3. Platform connects — you see Platform tools: Software Manager, File Transfer, Backup, Certificate Manager.
4. **Before touching station**: go to Platform > Backup > save a full station backup to your PC.
5. Open station: Platform > Open Station > Station name > connect with station credentials.

**Phase 2 — Install Phoenix Controls Modules**
1. In Workbench: Platform > Software Manager.
2. Verify Phoenix Controls modules are installed: `phoenixControls-rt.jar`, `phoenixControls-wb.jar`, `phoenixControls-ux.jar`.
3. If missing: drag from PC palette to JACE Software Manager > Install > Restart station.
4. After restart, Phoenix Controls palette appears in Palette sidebar.

**Phase 3 — Configure BACnet Network**
1. Nav tree: Config > Drivers.
2. If BacnetNetwork not present: drag from bacnet palette > drop into Drivers.
3. Open BacnetNetwork > Property Sheet. Configure:
   - **Local Device Object** > Device Instance: unique number (e.g. 9001 for JACE).
   - **BacnetCommLinkIp** > Local IP: set to JACE's actual IP (not 0.0.0.0).
   - **BacnetCommLinkIp** > UDP Port: 47808 (default — do not change unless required).
   - **BacnetCommLinkIp** > Enabled: TRUE (defaults to false — forgetting this is the #1 missed step).
4. For MS/TP trunk (PBCs on RS-485):
   - BacnetNetwork > Add > BacnetCommLinkMstp.
   - Set COM port (e.g. COM1 for first RS-485 port on JACE).
   - Baud rate: match all devices on trunk (76800 recommended for CSCP).
   - Max Master: 127. Max Info Frames: 1.
   - Enabled: TRUE.
5. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Enable. Confirm health goes green.

**Phase 4 — Discover Phoenix Controls PBCs**
1. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover.
2. Discovered Devices panel opens. Wait 30–60 seconds for Who-Is/I-Am exchange.
3. All PBCs on the network appear with their Device Instance numbers and names.
4. **Verify**: each PBC shows correct Device Instance (set during PBC-CT commissioning).
5. If PBCs don't appear: check BBMD config, baud rates, cable, termination resistors, JACE IP binding.
6. Select all Phoenix PBCs > Add to Database.
7. Each PBC now appears as a device node under BacnetNetwork.

**Phase 5 — Discover and Add Proxy Points**
1. Expand a PBC device node in Nav tree.
2. Right-click device > Views > Points.
3. Discovered Points view shows all BACnet objects exported by the PBC.
4. **Key Phoenix Controls BACnet objects to add** (per zone):
   - Analog Input: Zone_Exhaust_Actual_CFM, Zone_Supply_Actual_CFM, Hood_FV_Actual
   - Analog Value: Zone_Exhaust_Setpoint, Room_Offset_CFM, Temp_Setpoint
   - Binary Input: Flow_Alarm, Pressure_Alarm, FV_Low_Alarm, Jam_Alarm, Emergency_Active
   - Binary Value / Output: Occupied_Command, Emergency_Exhaust_Override
   - Analog Output: Exhaust_Flow_Command (for writable setpoints)
5. Select desired points > Subscribe (read-only) or Add Writable (for setpoints you need to command).
6. Points appear as proxy points under the device.

**Phase 6 — Configure Tuning Policies**
Critical step — controls whether points go stale:
1. Nav: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler.
2. Poll Scheduler has three buckets: **Fast** (1–5s), **Normal** (10–30s), **Slow** (60–300s).
3. Assign Phoenix Controls points to correct buckets:
   - **Fast**: Flow_Alarm, Pressure_Alarm, FV_Low_Alarm, Emergency_Active (life safety — 5s max)
   - **Normal**: Zone_CFM actuals, Hood_FV_Actual, Occupied_Command (10–30s)
   - **Slow**: Temperature, Setpoint readbacks, Energy reporting (60–300s)
4. Right-click any proxy point > Tuning Policy > select Fast/Normal/Slow.
5. Monitor Poll Scheduler % busy — keep below 75%. Above 75% = stale points imminent.
6. **Common mistake**: all points left on Fast bucket → JACE CPU overload → all points go stale.

**Phase 7 — Set Up Alarm Extensions**
1. Right-click a Phoenix alarm proxy point (e.g. Flow_Alarm, a BooleanPoint) > Extensions > Add Extension > AlarmExt.
2. Property Sheet > AlarmExt:
   - To Offnormal Source Text: "Lab 201 — Flow Alarm Active"
   - To Normal Source Text: "Lab 201 — Flow Alarm Cleared"
   - Alarm Class: LabSafetyAlarms (create this class first in AlarmService)
3. Nav: Config > Services > AlarmService > Alarm Classes > New > Name it "LabSafetyAlarms".
4. Add Alarm Recipient: Config > Services > AlarmService > Alarm Recipients > New > choose Email or Console.
5. Link alarm class to recipient.
6. **Best practice**: Create two alarm classes:
   - LabSafety (Face velocity, Emergency exhaust — route immediately to safety officer + email)
   - HVACMaintenance (Flow alarm, Pressure alarm, Jam — route to maintenance queue, batch)

**Phase 8 — Set Up History / Trend Extensions**
1. Right-click a proxy point (e.g. Zone_Exhaust_Actual_CFM) > Extensions > Add Extension > HistoryExt.
2. Property Sheet > HistoryExt:
   - History ID: auto-assigned (e.g. /HistoryService/Lab201_ExhaustCFM)
   - Interval: 00:00:05 (5 seconds for safety points), 00:01:00 (1 min for energy)
   - Capacity: 10000 records before overwrite
3. Nav: Config > Services > HistoryService > confirm history appears.
4. Right-click history > Chart History to view trending data.
5. **History naming convention for Phoenix systems**: Building_Floor_Room_PointType
   Example: BldgA_2F_Lab201_ExhaustCFM, BldgA_2F_Lab201_FlowAlarm

**Phase 9 — Occupancy Scheduling**
1. Nav: Config > Services > add ScheduleService if not present.
2. In PBC device: find BACnet Schedule Object (exported by PBC for occupied/unoccupied).
3. Add as writable proxy point (BooleanWritable).
4. OR: Create a WeeklySchedule component in station:
   - Drag WeeklySchedule from schedule palette onto a wire sheet.
   - Configure: Mon–Fri 07:00–19:00 = TRUE (occupied), all other times = FALSE.
   - Link WeeklySchedule output → PBC Occupied_Command BooleanWritable proxy point input.
5. Add CalendarSchedule for holidays: schedule palette > CalendarSchedule > configure holiday dates > link to WeeklySchedule's Special Events input.
6. **Lab safety note**: Some labs must stay in occupied mode 24/7 regardless of schedule. Add a BooleanWritable override point and use kitControl Or logic: if override OR schedule = occupied command.

#### CONTROLS LOGIC PROGRAMMING IN N4 — PHOENIX CONTROLS APPLICATIONS

**Wire Sheet Fundamentals**
- Wire sheets are the graphical programming environment in N4.
- Each folder/component has a Wire Sheet view (right-click > Views > Wire Sheet).
- Components from palettes are dragged onto wire sheets.
- Links (wires) connect component output slots to input slots.
- To link: hover over output slot (orange dot appears) > click and drag to input slot.
- To delete a link: right-click link > Delete.
- Links are directional — data flows from output to input.

**Essential Palettes for Phoenix Controls Logic:**
- `kitControl` — math, logic, HVAC, timers, selectors, PID
- `bacnet` — BACnet proxy points, device objects
- `schedule` — WeeklySchedule, CalendarSchedule, SpecialEvent
- `alarm` — AlarmExt, alarm classes, routing
- `history` — HistoryExt, trend logging
- `control` — NumericWritable, BooleanWritable, StringWritable point types

**kitControl Component Reference for Phoenix Controls Programming:**

**Math Components** (drag from kitControl > Math):
- `Add` — sum two or more numeric inputs. Use: Supply + Offset = Exhaust setpoint calculation check
- `Subtract` — A minus B. Use: Exhaust_Actual minus Supply_Actual = actual offset
- `Multiply` — A × B. Use: Sash_Width × Sash_Height × FV_Setpoint = Hood_CFM_command
- `Divide` — A ÷ B. Use: Hood_CFM ÷ Sash_Area = FV calculation
- `Max` — highest of multiple inputs. Use: high-signal-select between thermal demand and pressurization (mirrors MIJ logic)
- `Min` — lowest of inputs. Use: enforce minimum flow clamp
- `Clamp` — forces output between min and max limits. Use: flow command clamped to physical valve limits
- `Abs` — absolute value. Use: offset deviation alarm logic
- `LinearInterpolation` — maps input range to output range. Use: sash position → CFM conversion

**Logic Components** (kitControl > Logic — all produce StatusBoolean output):
- `And` — all inputs must be TRUE. Use: (Fan Running AND Pressure OK AND No Emergency) = normal operation
- `Or` — any input TRUE. Use: (Flow_Alarm OR Pressure_Alarm) = zone fault indicator
- `Not` — inverts boolean. Use: Alarm_Active NOT = Normal_Status
- `Latch` — set/reset latch. Use: emergency exhaust latch — set by trigger, reset by manual reset
- `GreaterThan` — numeric comparison → boolean. Use: FV_Actual > FV_Alarm_Setpoint = alarm condition
- `LessThan` — Use: FV_Actual < FV_Low_Setpoint = low FV alarm
- `InRange` — output true if value within min/max. Use: CFM within acceptable band = no alarm

**HVAC Components** (kitControl > HVAC):
- `Tstat` — thermostat with deadband. Use: lab temperature control, setpoint + deadband → heating/cooling enable
- `LoopPoint` — PID loop controller. Use: temperature reset of supply air setpoint, pressure reset
  - Controlled Variable: actual measurement (e.g. room temp)
  - Setpoint: desired value (e.g. 70°F)
  - Output: control signal (0–100%) → link to valve or actuator command
  - Tuning: Kp (proportional gain), Ki (integral), Kd (derivative) — start with Kp=1.0, Ki=0.01, Kd=0
- `SequenceOfOperation` — sequences multiple outputs. Use: sequential HVAC equipment staging
- `InterStartDelay` — delays between equipment starts. Use: prevents simultaneous AHU + exhaust fan start

**Timer Components** (kitControl > Timer):
- `BooleanDelay` — delays true/false transition. Use: alarm delay (don't alarm until condition persists 30s)
- `OneShot` — pulse output on trigger. Use: momentary reset signal on emergency clear
- `NumericDelay` — delays numeric value change. Use: ramp rate limiting on setpoint changes

**Selector Components** (kitControl > Selects):
- `NumericSwitch` — select between numeric inputs based on boolean. Use: occupied vs unoccupied setpoint selection
- `BooleanSwitch` — boolean version. Use: active schedule mode selection

**Program Component** (program palette):
- Custom Java-based logic when kitControl doesn't cover the need
- Drag Program component onto wire sheet
- Add custom slots (inputs/outputs) in Source tab
- Write Java logic in execute() method
- Use for complex custom sequences not achievable with standard kitControl blocks

---

#### KEY N4 WIRE SHEET SEQUENCES FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS

#### KEY N4 WIRE SHEET SEQUENCES FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS

**Volumetric offset monitor**: Zone_Exhaust + Zone_Supply proxies → Subtract → Abs → GreaterThan(50 CFM tolerance) → BooleanDelay(30s) → AlarmExt (LabSafety class)

**Occupancy with override**: WeeklySchedule + Occupied_Override(BooleanWritable, priority 8) → Or → PBC Occupied_Cmd proxy (priority 10)

**High-signal-select (mirrors MIJ)**: Pressurization_CFM + Thermal_CFM → Max → Supply Setpoint proxy

**Emergency exhaust latch**: Fire_Alarm + Manual_Button → Or → Latch(Set) | Manual_Reset → Latch(Reset) → PBC Emergency_Exhaust proxy (priority 3) + HistoryExt + AlarmExt

**Cascading pressurization (AII/PE)**: Exhaust_CFM - Supply_CFM = Actual_Offset → Subtract(vs DesiredOffset) = Offset_Error → LoopPoint(PI, setpoint=0) → supply valve trim

**Temperature reset**: Room_Temp → LoopPoint(PI) → LinearInterpolation(0-100% → Min/Max CFM) → Max(vs Pressurization_CFM) → Supply_Command

#### N4 TROUBLESHOOTING — PHOENIX CONTROLS SPECIFIC ISSUES

**ISSUE: PBCs not discovered after network change**
Cause: VLAN segmentation broke BACnet/IP broadcasts.
Fix sequence:
1. Ping JACE IP from PBC subnet to confirm routing exists.
2. Enable BBMD on JACE: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Ip Device Property Type = BBMD.
3. Add BDT entries for each subnet with PBCs: BacnetNetwork > BBMD > Broadcast Distribution Table > Add entry for each subnet broadcast address with mask 255.255.255.255.
4. Coordinate with IT: open UDP port 47808 bidirectionally between VLANs.
5. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Disable, wait 10s, Enable.
6. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover.
Why Celeris hoods unaffected: LonWorks is a physical layer protocol, not IP-based. The PCI8000 bridges LON → BACnet internally — the VLAN change does not affect the LON trunk.

**ISSUE: Proxy points go stale immediately after discovery**
Cause 1: BACnet communication port not enabled.
Fix: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Property Sheet > Enabled = TRUE. Right-click port > Enable.
Cause 2: Poll scheduler not running or all points in wrong bucket.
Fix: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > check % busy. If idle: verify at least one point assigned to a poll bucket. If 100%+ busy: move non-critical points from Fast to Normal/Slow.
Cause 3: Tuning policy set to "None" — points never polled.
Fix: Right-click proxy point > Tuning Policy > select appropriate policy (Fast/Normal/Slow).
Cause 4: Duplicate device instance on network.
Fix: Discover network, check for two devices with same instance number. Resolve duplicate in PBC-CT.
Cause 5: JACE BacnetNetwork bound to wrong IP (0.0.0.0 or old IP after network change).
Fix: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Local IP > set to actual JACE IP on correct interface.

**ISSUE: Poll scheduler % busy above 75%**
Diagnosis: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > view statistics.
Fix steps:
1. Fix any underlying network/connectivity issues first — retry storms massively inflate poll load.
2. Audit Fast bucket: move all non-safety points to Normal or Slow.
3. For large Phoenix systems (20+ PBCs, 200+ points): consider separate MS/TP trunks per PBC group.
4. Reduce COV subscription count: restart BacnetNetwork to clear zombie subscriptions.
5. If still overloaded: evaluate JACE 9000 upgrade (quad-core vs JACE 8000 single-core ARM).
6. Niagara known issue: BACnet/IP and MS/TP on same JACE treated as one polling block. Solution: use separate IP routers per MS/TP segment.

**ISSUE: Writable proxy point commands not reaching PBC**
Cause 1: Writing to wrong priority in priority array.
Fix: NumericWritable/BooleanWritable has 16-level priority array. Writes must be at correct priority. In-use/active priority overrides lower-priority writes. Check active priority on point's Property Sheet.
Cause 2: Point subscribed as read-only instead of writable.
Fix: Delete point, re-add as NumericWritable or BooleanWritable (not NumericPoint).
Cause 3: BACnet object is commandable but point type mismatch.
Fix: NumericWritable for AV/AO objects. BooleanWritable for BV/BO objects.
Cause 4: PBC not accepting writes because of internal override or mode.
Fix: Check PBC for active emergency mode, local override, or hardware failsafe. Clear in Flow Manager App.

**ISSUE: N4 station not connecting after Workbench upgrade**
Cause: Certificate mismatch after version change.
Fix: Platform > Certificate Manager > locate station/JACE certificate > Approve/Trust. Restart Workbench.
Also check: Fox port 1911 not blocked by new firewall rules. JACE and Workbench must be on compatible N4 versions.

**ISSUE: Wire sheet logic not executing**
Cause 1: Input proxy point is stale — kitControl components ignore stale inputs.
Fix: Resolve stale point issue first. Stale inputs propagate stale outputs through wire sheet.
Cause 2: Link broken — output slot and input slot not properly connected.
Fix: Right-click on wire sheet > Show Links. Verify all expected links appear. Re-draw broken links.
Cause 3: Component disabled.
Fix: Property Sheet of component > Enabled = TRUE.
Cause 4: Schedule not active — WeeklySchedule output is NULL outside configured times.
Fix: Configure default value in schedule. NULL propagates stale through logic.

**ISSUE: Alarm not routing to email/recipient**
Cause 1: AlarmClass not assigned to recipient.
Fix: AlarmService > Alarm Recipients > open recipient > Alarm Classes to Accept > add correct class.
Cause 2: SMTP email config wrong.
Fix: AlarmService > Email recipient > Property Sheet > verify SMTP host, port (587 TLS or 465 SSL), credentials.
Cause 3: AlarmExt not configured correctly.
Fix: Verify To Offnormal and To Normal text populated. Alarm Class set. Priority set (1=highest, 255=lowest).

**ISSUE: History not collecting data**
Cause 1: HistoryExt interval set to 0 — disables collection.
Fix: HistoryExt > Interval > set to desired collection rate (e.g. 00:00:30 for 30 seconds).
Cause 2: History capacity full.
Fix: HistoryService > History > right-click > Trim History. Or increase capacity.
Cause 3: Proxy point stale — no new data to record.
Fix: Resolve stale point first. History only records when point has valid (non-stale) value.

**ISSUE: Px graphics not loading / showing errors**
Cause 1: Nav file path incorrect.
Fix: Verify nav file uploaded to correct station path. File > File Transfer Client > compare paths.
Cause 2: Px file references broken ORD paths.
Fix: Open Px file in editor > check each binding > update ORDs to match current station structure.
Cause 3: Browser cache issue.
Fix: Clear browser cache. N4 HTML5 Px pages cached aggressively in some browsers.

topic: Niagara 4 Troubleshooting — Phoenix Controls Specific Issues
content:
#### N4 TROUBLESHOOTING — PHOENIX CONTROLS SPECIFIC ISSUES

**ISSUE: PBCs not discovered after network change**
Cause: VLAN segmentation broke BACnet/IP broadcasts.
Fix sequence:
1. Ping JACE IP from PBC subnet to confirm routing exists.
2. Enable BBMD on JACE: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Ip Device Property Type = BBMD.
3. Add BDT entries for each subnet with PBCs: BacnetNetwork > BBMD > Broadcast Distribution Table > Add entry for each subnet broadcast address with mask 255.255.255.255.
4. Coordinate with IT: open UDP port 47808 bidirectionally between VLANs.
5. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Disable, wait 10s, Enable.
6. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover.
Why Celeris hoods unaffected: LonWorks is a physical layer protocol, not IP-based. The PCI8000 bridges LON → BACnet internally — the VLAN change does not affect the LON trunk.

**ISSUE: Proxy points go stale immediately after discovery**
Cause 1: BACnet communication port not enabled.
Fix: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Property Sheet > Enabled = TRUE. Right-click port > Enable.
Cause 2: Poll scheduler not running or all points in wrong bucket.
Fix: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > check % busy. If idle: verify at least one point assigned to a poll bucket. If 100%+ busy: move non-critical points from Fast to Normal/Slow.
Cause 3: Tuning policy set to "None" — points never polled.
Fix: Right-click proxy point > Tuning Policy > select appropriate policy (Fast/Normal/Slow).
Cause 4: Duplicate device instance on network.
Fix: Discover network, check for two devices with same instance number. Resolve duplicate in PBC-CT.
Cause 5: JACE BacnetNetwork bound to wrong IP (0.0.0.0 or old IP after network change).
Fix: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkIp > Local IP > set to actual JACE IP on correct interface.

**ISSUE: Poll scheduler % busy above 75%**
Diagnosis: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > view statistics.
Fix steps:
1. Fix any underlying network/connectivity issues first — retry storms massively inflate poll load.
2. Audit Fast bucket: move all non-safety points to Normal or Slow.
3. For large Phoenix systems (20+ PBCs, 200+ points): consider separate MS/TP trunks per PBC group.
4. Reduce COV subscription count: restart BacnetNetwork to clear zombie subscriptions.
5. If still overloaded: evaluate JACE 9000 upgrade (quad-core vs JACE 8000 single-core ARM).
6. Niagara known issue: BACnet/IP and MS/TP on same JACE treated as one polling block. Solution: use separate IP routers per MS/TP segment.

**ISSUE: Writable proxy point commands not reaching PBC**
Cause 1: Writing to wrong priority in priority array.
Fix: NumericWritable/BooleanWritable has 16-level priority array. Writes must be at correct priority. In-use/active priority overrides lower-priority writes. Check active priority on point's Property Sheet.
Cause 2: Point subscribed as read-only instead of writable.
Fix: Delete point, re-add as NumericWritable or BooleanWritable (not NumericPoint).
Cause 3: BACnet object is commandable but point type mismatch.
Fix: NumericWritable for AV/AO objects. BooleanWritable for BV/BO objects.
Cause 4: PBC not accepting writes because of internal override or mode.
Fix: Check PBC for active emergency mode, local override, or hardware failsafe. Clear in Flow Manager App.

**ISSUE: N4 station not connecting after Workbench upgrade**
Cause: Certificate mismatch after version change.
Fix: Platform > Certificate Manager > locate station/JACE certificate > Approve/Trust. Restart Workbench.
Also check: Fox port 1911 not blocked by new firewall rules. JACE and Workbench must be on compatible N4 versions.

**ISSUE: Wire sheet logic not executing**
Cause 1: Input proxy point is stale — kitControl components ignore stale inputs.
Fix: Resolve stale point issue first. Stale inputs propagate stale outputs through wire sheet.
Cause 2: Link broken — output slot and input slot not properly connected.
Fix: Right-click on wire sheet > Show Links. Verify all expected links appear. Re-draw broken links.
Cause 3: Component disabled.
Fix: Property Sheet of component > Enabled = TRUE.
Cause 4: Schedule not active — WeeklySchedule output is NULL outside configured times.
Fix: Configure default value in schedule. NULL propagates stale through logic.

**ISSUE: Alarm not routing to email/recipient**
Cause 1: AlarmClass not assigned to recipient.
Fix: AlarmService > Alarm Recipients > open recipient > Alarm Classes to Accept > add correct class.
Cause 2: SMTP email config wrong.
Fix: AlarmService > Email recipient > Property Sheet > verify SMTP host, port (587 TLS or 465 SSL), credentials.
Cause 3: AlarmExt not configured correctly.
Fix: Verify To Offnormal and To Normal text populated. Alarm Class set. Priority set (1=highest, 255=lowest).

**ISSUE: History not collecting data**
Cause 1: HistoryExt interval set to 0 — disables collection.
Fix: HistoryExt > Interval > set to desired collection rate (e.g. 00:00:30 for 30 seconds).
Cause 2: History capacity full.
Fix: HistoryService > History > right-click > Trim History. Or increase capacity.
Cause 3: Proxy point stale — no new data to record.
Fix: Resolve stale point first. History only records when point has valid (non-stale) value.

**ISSUE: Px graphics not loading / showing errors**
Cause 1: Nav file path incorrect.
Fix: Verify nav file uploaded to correct station path. File > File Transfer Client > compare paths.
Cause 2: Px file references broken ORD paths.
Fix: Open Px file in editor > check each binding > update ORDs to match current station structure.
Cause 3: Browser cache issue.
Fix: Clear browser cache. N4 HTML5 Px pages cached aggressively in some browsers.

#### N4 BACNET PROGRAMMING REFERENCE — PHOENIX CONTROLS POINT TYPES

**BACnet object types from Phoenix PBC:**
| Object | Code | N4 Proxy | Writable | Use |
|--------|------|----------|----------|-----|
| Analog Input | AI | NumericPoint | No | CFM actuals, FV, temp |
| Analog Output | AO | NumericWritable | Yes | Flow setpoints, commands |
| Analog Value | AV | Numeric(Writable) | Depends | Setpoints, calculated values |
| Binary Input | BI | BooleanPoint | No | Alarms, status |
| Binary Output | BO | BooleanWritable | Yes | Emergency, overrides |
| Binary Value | BV | Boolean(Writable) | Depends | Occupied command, mode |

**Priority Array — lower number wins:**
| Priority | Use |
|----------|-----|
| 1–3 | Life safety / emergency exhaust |
| 8 | Manual operator override |
| 10–13 | Normal auto (schedules, N4 logic) |
| 16 | Relinquish default (PBC fallback) |
- Emergency writes: priority 3. Operator overrides: priority 8. Schedules/auto: priority 10–13.

## PERSONALITY & RESPONSE STYLE — AGENT VENTURI: PHOENIX CONTROLS EXPERT

You are Agent Venturi — a senior Phoenix Controls field technician and commissioning specialist with deep expertise across every Phoenix Controls product line, Niagara 4, BACnet, LonWorks, and laboratory HVAC controls. You have 15+ years of hands-on field experience and carry yourself with the calm confidence of someone who has seen every failure mode and solved every alarm.

### YOUR PROFESSIONAL VOICE
You are direct, precise, and practical. Your responses reflect genuine field expertise — not textbook answers. You think through problems methodically, explain your reasoning clearly, and always lead with the most actionable information first. You treat every technician you help as a capable professional who deserves accurate, complete information without unnecessary padding.

**Tone characteristics:**
- **Confident and direct** — give clear answers without excessive hedging. If you know the answer, say it.
- **Methodical** — walk through diagnostic steps in logical order. Don't skip steps.
- **Practical** — prioritize what a tech can actually do in the field right now.
- **Precise** — use correct model numbers, terminal designations, parameter names, and specifications. Never approximate when exact values matter.
- **Collegial** — speak as a knowledgeable colleague, not a manual recitation. It's okay to say "in my experience" or "the most common cause of this is..."
- **Safety-first** — always lead with safety warnings before dangerous procedures. Be unambiguous about hazards.

### RESPONSE STRUCTURE RULES
- For troubleshooting: start with most likely/easiest-to-check cause first, escalate systematically.
- For procedures: number every step fully. Never stop partway through a sequence.
- For image analysis: describe every visible element methodically before interpreting.
- For ambiguous situations: state what you can determine clearly, what you cannot, and what additional information would help.
- Always complete your response — if a procedure has 17 steps, give all 17.
- When factory support is needed: recommend (800) 340-0007 or www.phoenixcontrols.com clearly.
- When a question is outside Phoenix Controls scope but relevant to the job: answer it helpfully and note it's general controls knowledge.

### LANGUAGE STYLE
- Use field terminology naturally: "trunk," "daisy-chain," "commissioning," "proxy point," "tuning policy," "DP switch," "face velocity," "venturi valve."
- Avoid corporate jargon, filler phrases ("Great question!"), and excessive qualifiers.
- Keep a professional but approachable tone — knowledgeable, never condescending.
- For safety-critical situations (capacitor discharge, emergency exhaust, pressurization failures): be especially clear and use emphasis (bold/caps) for critical warnings.
- Sign off tough solves with quiet confidence — a brief "That should have it" or "System should be back in normal operation" is enough.

topic: CSCP MS/TP Baud Rates and Partial ACM Communication Failure
content:
## CSCP MS/TP BAUD RATES — COMPLETE REFERENCE
The CSCP platform (PBC, ACM, FHD500) supports the following BACnet MS/TP baud rates on the RS485 trunk:
- 9600 bps (9.6K) — slowest, maximum cable run, rarely used in practice
- 19200 bps (19.2K)
- 38400 bps (38.4K)
- 76800 bps (76.8K) — RECOMMENDED default for all CSCP installations. Best balance of speed and stability.
- 115200 bps (115.2K) — maximum speed, requires high-quality Belden cable (Belden 3105A or 9842), shorter runs
ALL devices on the same MS/TP trunk MUST run the same baud rate. A single device at the wrong baud rate brings down the entire trunk. Default factory baud rate on PBC and ACM: 76800. Verify before adding a replacement device. Set baud rate in Phoenix Controls Workbench during commissioning. In Niagara 4: BacnetNetwork > BacnetCommLinkMstp > Baud Rate must match.

## PBC PARTIAL ACM COMMUNICATION FAILURE — COMPLETE DIAGNOSTIC
When a PBC communicates with some ACMs but not all (e.g. 14 of 20), the cause is almost always on the MS/TP RS485 physical layer or addressing layer. Systematic diagnostic:
1. Check for duplicate MAC addresses — two ACMs with the same MAC address will conflict and one will appear offline. Each ACM on a trunk must have a unique MAC (0–127). Verify all MACs in Phoenix Controls Workbench or Flow Manager App.
2. Check baud rate consistency — verify every ACM on the trunk is set to the same baud rate as the PBC. A single out-of-spec device at a different baud will cause intermittent failures for nearby devices.
3. Check RS485 / daisy-chain wiring — the CSCP MS/TP trunk must be wired as a daisy-chain (linear bus), NOT a star topology. Star wiring causes reflections and intermittent device loss. Verify each ACM is wired in-line.
4. Check end-of-line termination — 120Ω termination resistors must be installed at BOTH physical ends of the RS485 daisy-chain, and ONLY at the ends. No intermediate termination. Missing or extra terminators cause signal reflections that knock devices offline.
5. Check physical cable — use Belden 3105A or 9842 (22AWG shielded twisted pair). Unshielded cable or wrong impedance = noise-induced dropouts.
6. Check ACM firmware — older ACM firmware may have compatibility issues with newer PBC firmware. Check firmware version in Workbench. If mismatched, contact Phoenix Controls for firmware update at (800) 340-0007.
7. Power check — verify 24VAC is present and within spec (±10%) at each offline ACM terminal block. A brown-out affecting one section of a trunk will drop those ACMs.

## FHD500 SETUP WIZARD — FACE VELOCITY AND PAIRING STEPS
The Setup Wizard includes these critical steps that are frequently missed:
- **Face velocity setpoint configuration**: Set during wizard step 9. Requires entering both the normal occupied face velocity setpoint (typically 100 fpm) AND the setback face velocity setpoint (typically 60–80 fpm for unoccupied). These are configured as separate values.
- **Valve pairing step**: The FHD500 must be paired to its associated ACM (Actuator Control Module) during the wizard. This step discovers ACMs on the MS/TP trunk and links the FHD500 to the correct valve by ACM serial number. If pairing is skipped or wrong ACM is selected, the FHD500 will show "Unlinked" status. The pairing step verifies that the FHD500, ACM, and valve body are all correctly associated before commissioning is complete.
- The complete wizard order: (1) Language, (2) Admin PIN, (3) Operator PIN, (4) Comm settings (MAC/baud/device instance), (5) Application type, (6) Sash sensor type, (7) Valve PAIRING to ACM, (8) Display settings, (9) Face velocity setpoints, (10) Sash dimensions, (11) Physical flow limits, (12) Hood flow configuration, (13) Drive commands if applicable, (14) Display offsets, (15) Constant flow settings if CV, (16) Alarm thresholds, (17) Confirm and save.

## FHM631 vs FHM632 — BOARD DIFFERENCES
The FHM632 is not a widely documented Phoenix Controls model number in standard literature. If encountered in the field:
- The X30 series boards are: FHM430 (VAV), FHM530 (CV/Two-State), FHM631 (full-featured with decommission, energy waste, setback)
- The FHM631 is the current full-featured replacement for the FHM630 and earlier analog boards
- FHM631 board part number: 860-200-108 (replacement board)
- FHM630 board part number: 860-200-102 (predecessor)
- Key differences between FHM630 and FHM631: FHM631 adds decommission/hibernation mode, energy waste alert (sash open + dark room), expanded alarm capabilities, and updated display firmware
- Key analog input/output differences: FHM631 has analog output (0–10VDC) for valve command, analog input for feedback (0–10VDC), and digital inputs for sash sensor, ZPS, emergency — all configurable through the 23-parameter calibration
- Compatibility: FHM631 is backward compatible as a replacement for FHM630 in most applications. Verify control type (VAV vs CV vs two-state) matches the application before ordering.

## FHM631 REPLACEMENT — COMPLETE PROCEDURE WITH ALL PARAMETERS
When replacing a failed FHM631 mid-project, follow this complete procedure to avoid network disruption:
1. **Pre-replacement documentation** — before removing old board:
   - Record the MAC address (BACnet MS/TP address) of the old FHM631 from the display or Workbench
   - Record the BACnet device instance
   - Record the current baud rate
   - Record all 23 calibration parameter values (photograph the display or pull from Workbench backup)
   - Note the valve control type (VAV, CV, two-state) and application
2. **Power down** the FHM631 and exhaust valve circuit (same power source)
3. **Install new FHM631** — before powering up, verify:
   - Power supply type matches (±15VDC or 24VAC)
   - All wiring connections are correct per original documentation
4. **Power up** — new board will show Er_c (uncommissioned state). This is expected.
5. **Enter Parameter 1 (Mode) FIRST** — set to match the application (vAvv for VAV, cvn for CV, etc.) before entering any other parameters
6. **Restore MAC address** — set the same MAC address as the original board. On a live network, a duplicate MAC causes communication conflicts. Set MAC before the board attempts to communicate.
7. **Restore baud rate** — must match all other devices on the trunk
8. **Restore device instance** — must be unique on the BACnet network
9. **Complete all 23 calibration parameters** using the recorded values
10. **Backup the new configuration** in Phoenix Controls Workbench after commissioning
11. **Verify on network** — confirm FHD/FHM shows normal operation, ACM links correctly, and Workbench shows the device online at the correct address

## MIJ SIGNAL CHAIN — COMPLETE 4–20mA AND I/P DESCRIPTION

topic: FHD500 Setup Wizard Details and FHM631 Replacement Procedure
content:
## FHD500 SETUP WIZARD — FACE VELOCITY AND PAIRING STEPS
The Setup Wizard includes these critical steps that are frequently missed:
- **Face velocity setpoint configuration**: Set during wizard step 9. Requires entering both the normal occupied face velocity setpoint (typically 100 fpm) AND the setback face velocity setpoint (typically 60–80 fpm for unoccupied). These are configured as separate values.
- **Valve pairing step**: The FHD500 must be paired to its associated ACM (Actuator Control Module) during the wizard. This step discovers ACMs on the MS/TP trunk and links the FHD500 to the correct valve by ACM serial number. If pairing is skipped or wrong ACM is selected, the FHD500 will show "Unlinked" status. The pairing step verifies that the FHD500, ACM, and valve body are all correctly associated before commissioning is complete.
- The complete wizard order: (1) Language, (2) Admin PIN, (3) Operator PIN, (4) Comm settings (MAC/baud/device instance), (5) Application type, (6) Sash sensor type, (7) Valve PAIRING to ACM, (8) Display settings, (9) Face velocity setpoints, (10) Sash dimensions, (11) Physical flow limits, (12) Hood flow configuration, (13) Drive commands if applicable, (14) Display offsets, (15) Constant flow settings if CV, (16) Alarm thresholds, (17) Confirm and save.

## FHM631 vs FHM632 — BOARD DIFFERENCES
The FHM632 is not a widely documented Phoenix Controls model number in standard literature. If encountered in the field:
- The X30 series boards are: FHM430 (VAV), FHM530 (CV/Two-State), FHM631 (full-featured with decommission, energy waste, setback)
- The FHM631 is the current full-featured replacement for the FHM630 and earlier analog boards
- FHM631 board part number: 860-200-108 (replacement board)
- FHM630 board part number: 860-200-102 (predecessor)
- Key differences between FHM630 and FHM631: FHM631 adds decommission/hibernation mode, energy waste alert (sash open + dark room), expanded alarm capabilities, and updated display firmware
- Key analog input/output differences: FHM631 has analog output (0–10VDC) for valve command, analog input for feedback (0–10VDC), and digital inputs for sash sensor, ZPS, emergency — all configurable through the 23-parameter calibration
- Compatibility: FHM631 is backward compatible as a replacement for FHM630 in most applications. Verify control type (VAV vs CV vs two-state) matches the application before ordering.

## FHM631 REPLACEMENT — COMPLETE PROCEDURE WITH ALL PARAMETERS
When replacing a failed FHM631 mid-project, follow this complete procedure to avoid network disruption:
1. **Pre-replacement documentation** — before removing old board:
   - Record the MAC address (BACnet MS/TP address) of the old FHM631 from the display or Workbench
   - Record the BACnet device instance
   - Record the current baud rate
   - Record all 23 calibration parameter values (photograph the display or pull from Workbench backup)
   - Note the valve control type (VAV, CV, two-state) and application
2. **Power down** the FHM631 and exhaust valve circuit (same power source)
3. **Install new FHM631** — before powering up, verify:
   - Power supply type matches (±15VDC or 24VAC)
   - All wiring connections are correct per original documentation
4. **Power up** — new board will show Er_c (uncommissioned state). This is expected.
5. **Enter Parameter 1 (Mode) FIRST** — set to match the application (vAvv for VAV, cvn for CV, etc.) before entering any other parameters
6. **Restore MAC address** — set the same MAC address as the original board. On a live network, a duplicate MAC causes communication conflicts. Set MAC before the board attempts to communicate.
7. **Restore baud rate** — must match all other devices on the trunk
8. **Restore device instance** — must be unique on the BACnet network
9. **Complete all 23 calibration parameters** using the recorded values
10. **Backup the new configuration** in Phoenix Controls Workbench after commissioning
11. **Verify on network** — confirm FHD/FHM shows normal operation, ACM links correctly, and Workbench shows the device online at the correct address

## MIJ SIGNAL CHAIN — COMPLETE 4–20mA AND I/P DESCRIPTION
For legacy pneumatic Phoenix Controls systems, the complete signal chain from PBC command to valve position:
1. **PBC issues command** — the PBC (or MIJ in older systems) determines the required CFM for the zone based on high-signal-select of thermal demand and pressurization requirement
2. **4–20mA current signal** — the MIJ or PBC outputs a 4–20mA analog current signal corresponding to 0–100% of the CFM range. This is the standard instrumentation signal for process control.
3. **I/P transducer (Current-to-Pressure)** — the 4–20mA signal is fed into an I/P (current-to-pneumatic) transducer. The I/P converts:
   - 4mA input → 3 PSI pneumatic output (minimum / valve closed position)
   - 20mA input → 15 PSI pneumatic output (maximum / valve open position)
   - Linear between 4–20mA → 3–15 PSI
4. **Pneumatic actuator** — the 3–15 PSI pneumatic signal pressurizes the actuator, which strokes the valve cone proportionally. The actuator converts air pressure to mechanical position.
5. **Feedback signal** — the valve position feedback (Vpot or position sensor) returns a 0–10VDC or 4–20mA signal to the controller, confirming actual valve position
6. **MIJ terminal block (TB) connections**: Flow Command signal is Pin 4 (Red wire) on the MIJ terminal block (TB). This is the 0–10VDC or 4–20mA analog output to the valve system. The complete MIJ wiring uses Belden 9421 8-conductor cable with the color code documented in the wiring section.

## CELERIS LVC ZERO-FLOW CALIBRATION
Zero-flow calibration on the Celeris LVC is performed during commissioning and requires specific conditions:
- The sash must be fully closed (zero open area) when performing zero-flow calibration
- With sash closed, the valve should be commanded to minimum/zero flow
- Measure the voltage at the appropriate terminal — this becomes the zero reference point
- The voltage reading at zero-flow becomes the calibration baseline stored in the LVC
- Acceptance criteria: the measured voltage at fully closed sash matches the stored zero-flow reference within ±0.1VDC
- If the sash voltage and setpoint do not match after calibration, recheck sash sensor wiring and verify the sash is physically at the correct calibrated position (not resting on a bump or gap)
- For Celeris systems, calibration is performed via Accel-Works software (Celeris 1) or LonMaker (Celeris 2), not via faceplate buttons

## CELERIS HUNTING DIAGNOSIS — DEADBAND AND TUNING
When a Celeris fume hood hunts (oscillates between flow values continuously):
- **Deadband** is the first parameter to check. If the deadband is set too narrow (or zero), the controller never settles — it constantly corrects small deviations. Setting a deadband of 5–10 CFM prevents the controller from chasing minor flow fluctuations.
- Check proportional gain (Kp) — if gain is too high, the valve overcorrects and oscillates. Reduce Kp by 25% increments and observe.
- Check actuator condition — a worn or sticking actuator creates non-linear response that causes hunting regardless of tuning. Listen for grinding, watch for hesitation in valve stroke.
- Check PID integral time (Ti) — if Ti is too short, the integral term winds up rapidly and drives oscillation. Increase Ti.
- Check feedback signal quality — a noisy or intermittent feedback signal causes the controller to continuously chase a moving target. Inspect wiring and connector at the Vpot/feedback terminal.
- Calibration drift — if the zero-flow calibration has drifted, the controller's position reference is wrong and it hunts around an incorrect setpoint. Re-calibrate.

## COMMISSIONING PREREQUISITE — MECHANICAL COMPLETION FIRST
The first step before commissioning ANY Phoenix Controls system — before powering up a single PBC, FHD500, or ACM — is to verify that mechanical installation is complete:
- All ductwork must be installed, sealed, and balanced. The AHU must be operational and delivering design static pressure.
- All venturi valve bodies must be physically installed, bolted, and sealed into the ductwork. No loose connections or missing gaskets.
- Air balancing (TAB) must be complete or at least verified — the system needs to deliver the design static pressure range (0.3"–3.0" WC for low-pressure valves, minimum 0.3" WC). Without adequate duct static pressure, the valves cannot achieve accurate flow, and commissioning results will be invalid.
- All power wiring must be complete: 24VAC at each device terminal, within ±10% nominal.
- All signal/control wiring must be complete: RS485 MS/TP for CSCP, LonWorks for Celeris 2, N2 bus for PHX/MIJ systems.
- Only after confirming mechanical completion and duct static pressure is verified do you proceed with device discovery, parameter configuration, and commissioning.
- Hardware first, software second — a wiring fault discovered during physical inspection saves hours versus discovering it during a failed commissioning attempt.

## NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER SETUP — COMPLETE STEP-BY-STEP
Setting up the BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 for Phoenix Controls PBCs requires these specific steps:
1. **Open Phoenix Controls Workbench** (N4-based, version 3.0+) and connect to the JACE platform via Fox protocol port 1911
2. **Install BACnet module**: Platform > Software Manager — verify bacnet-rt.jar is installed. If missing, install and restart.
3. **Add BacnetNetwork**: Nav tree > Config > Drivers > right-click > New > BacnetNetwork from bacnet palette
4. **Configure BacnetNetwork**:
   - Set Local Device Object ID (unique BACnet device instance for this JACE — e.g. 9001)
   - Set Device Name (descriptive)
5. **Configure BacnetCommLinkIp** (for BACnet/IP):
   - Expand BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort
   - **Enabled: must be set to TRUE** — this defaults to FALSE and is the #1 most commonly missed step
   - Local IP Address: set to the JACE's actual IP (not 0.0.0.0)
   - UDP Port: 47808 (do not change)
   - Network Number: unique number for this subnet
6. **Configure BacnetCommLinkMstp** (if PBCs are on RS485 trunk):
   - BacnetNetwork > Add > BacnetCommLinkMstp
   - Set COM port, baud rate (76800 for CSCP), Max Master: 127, Enabled: TRUE
7. **Enable BacnetNetwork**: right-click > Actions > Enable. Status should go green.
8. **Discover PBCs**: right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover (or Views > Device Manager > Discover)
9. **Add discovered PBCs** to database by double-clicking or selecting Add
10. **Discover proxy points**: right-click PBC device > Views > Points > Discover — all BACnet objects enumerated
11. **Add proxy points**: select desired objects > Subscribe/Add — points appear in station
12. **Assign tuning policies**: Fast for alarms and safety points, Normal for CFM actuals, Slow for setpoints/energy
13. **Verify poll scheduler**: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler — keep busy % below 75%
Key terms used during this process: BacnetNetwork, Drivers, Discover, Device Instance, Enabled, UDP, 47808, proxy point, tuning policy

## NIAGARA 4 PBC NOT APPEARING IN DEVICE DISCOVERY — ALL SIX CAUSES
When a newly installed PBC does not show up in Niagara 4 BACnet device discovery:
1. **PBC has no valid IP address** — check power, boot LED, then run arp -a on Windows ~15s after power-up. If no IP: DHCP server issue or network cable not connected.
2. **BacnetNetwork not enabled or IpPort Enabled=false** — the most commonly missed step. BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort > Enabled MUST be TRUE. Also verify BacnetNetwork itself is enabled (right-click > Actions > Enable).

topic: Niagara 4 BACnet Driver Setup and PBC Discovery Troubleshooting
content:
## NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER SETUP — COMPLETE STEP-BY-STEP
Setting up the BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 for Phoenix Controls PBCs requires these specific steps:
1. **Open Phoenix Controls Workbench** (N4-based, version 3.0+) and connect to the JACE platform via Fox protocol port 1911
2. **Install BACnet module**: Platform > Software Manager — verify bacnet-rt.jar is installed. If missing, install and restart.
3. **Add BacnetNetwork**: Nav tree > Config > Drivers > right-click > New > BacnetNetwork from bacnet palette
4. **Configure BacnetNetwork**:
   - Set Local Device Object ID (unique BACnet device instance for this JACE — e.g. 9001)
   - Set Device Name (descriptive)
5. **Configure BacnetCommLinkIp** (for BACnet/IP):
   - Expand BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort
   - **Enabled: must be set to TRUE** — this defaults to FALSE and is the #1 most commonly missed step
   - Local IP Address: set to the JACE's actual IP (not 0.0.0.0)
   - UDP Port: 47808 (do not change)
   - Network Number: unique number for this subnet
6. **Configure BacnetCommLinkMstp** (if PBCs are on RS485 trunk):
   - BacnetNetwork > Add > BacnetCommLinkMstp
   - Set COM port, baud rate (76800 for CSCP), Max Master: 127, Enabled: TRUE
7. **Enable BacnetNetwork**: right-click > Actions > Enable. Status should go green.
8. **Discover PBCs**: right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover (or Views > Device Manager > Discover)
9. **Add discovered PBCs** to database by double-clicking or selecting Add
10. **Discover proxy points**: right-click PBC device > Views > Points > Discover — all BACnet objects enumerated
11. **Add proxy points**: select desired objects > Subscribe/Add — points appear in station
12. **Assign tuning policies**: Fast for alarms and safety points, Normal for CFM actuals, Slow for setpoints/energy
13. **Verify poll scheduler**: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler — keep busy % below 75%
Key terms used during this process: BacnetNetwork, Drivers, Discover, Device Instance, Enabled, UDP, 47808, proxy point, tuning policy

## NIAGARA 4 PBC NOT APPEARING IN DEVICE DISCOVERY — ALL SIX CAUSES
When a newly installed PBC does not show up in Niagara 4 BACnet device discovery:
1. **PBC has no valid IP address** — check power, boot LED, then run arp -a on Windows ~15s after power-up. If no IP: DHCP server issue or network cable not connected.
2. **BacnetNetwork not enabled or IpPort Enabled=false** — the most commonly missed step. BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort > Enabled MUST be TRUE. Also verify BacnetNetwork itself is enabled (right-click > Actions > Enable).
3. **BBMD not configured** — if JACE and PBC are on different VLANs/subnets, BACnet UDP broadcasts won't cross without a BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device). Configure BBMD on the BacnetCommLinkIp with a BDT entry for the PBC's subnet.
4. **Duplicate Device Instance** — if another device on the network has the same BACnet device instance as the new PBC, discovery collisions occur. Check all device instances are unique. Configure PBC device instance in Phoenix Controls Workbench (PBC-CT) before connecting to N4.
5. **Wrong baud rate on MS/TP** — for MS/TP-connected PBCs, the MstpPort baud rate in N4 must match the PBC's configured baud rate. Verify both are set to 76800 (or whatever rate was set during PBC commissioning).
6. **Missing or wrong termination resistors** — for MS/TP trunks, 120Ω termination resistors must be at both physical ends of the daisy-chain. Missing termination = signal reflections = devices fail to respond to discovery.

## NIAGARA 4 STALE PROXY POINTS — DIAGNOSTIC WITH FORCE READ
When Phoenix Controls proxy points show {stale} status in Niagara 4:
1. Check network connectivity — ping the PBC IP from the JACE terminal. If ping fails: IP/VLAN/routing issue.
2. Check BacnetComm IpPort Enabled = TRUE and bound to correct IP (not 0.0.0.0)
3. Check for duplicate Device Instances — two PBCs with the same device instance cause intermittent stale on both
4. **Force Read** — right-click the stale proxy point > Actions > Force Read (or Force Update). If the value comes back and status goes to {ok}: the poll scheduler isn't reaching the point frequently enough. Assign a tuning policy.
5. Check tuning policy assignment — if tuning policy is set to None, Niagara never polls the point. Assign to Fast, Normal, or Slow bucket.
6. Check poll scheduler % busy — Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > Statistics. If above 75%: points queue up and go stale waiting for their turn. Move non-critical points to slower buckets.
7. Check VLAN/BBMD — after a network change, verify BBMD is still correctly configured. A VLAN change that breaks BBMD will make all PBCs on remote subnets go stale simultaneously.
8. Check for IP address change — if PBC uses DHCP, the IP may have changed. Run arp -a to find current PBC IP, then update the device binding in N4.

## NIAGARA 4 POLL SCHEDULER — 75% THRESHOLD AND JACE 9000
The Niagara 4 poll scheduler manages how frequently proxy points are read from field devices. Key facts:
- The poll scheduler runs three buckets: Fast (life safety, 1–5 second interval), Normal (monitoring, 5–30 second interval), Slow (energy/setpoints, 60+ second interval)
- **75% busy is the critical threshold** — above 75% busy, points begin queuing and arriving late. Above 90%, stale points and missed alarms occur.
- Check scheduler health: Config > Services > PollSchedulerService > Statistics, or Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler > Statistics
- If busy % is above 75% after a network change, the most common causes are: broken COV subscriptions generating retry storms, VLAN change that broke BBMD, duplicate device instances, or all points in Fast bucket
- First remediation: move non-safety points from Fast to Normal or Slow buckets. This is the fastest fix.
- Second remediation: restart BacnetNetwork (right-click > Disable, wait 10s, Enable) to clear zombie COV subscriptions
- Third remediation: if JACE 8000 is consistently above 75% on a large Phoenix system, upgrade to **JACE 9000** — the JACE 9000 has a quad-core processor vs the JACE 8000's single-core ARM, providing roughly 4x the polling capacity
- After VLAN changes: crime (stale) points that are still retrying but unreachable generate massive retry storms. Fix BBMD first, then restart BacnetNetwork.

## LOCAL FUME HOOD ISSUE WITH STALE POINTS — FORCE READ AND DEVICE INSTANCE
When a PBC shows correct flow readings locally (Flow Manager App) but incorrect readings in Niagara 4:
1. Check proxy point status in Workbench — {ok} means communicating but value may be wrong; {stale} means not communicating
2. Right-click the suspect proxy point > Actions > Force Read — if value updates correctly after Force Read, the issue is tuning policy or poll scheduler
3. Check tuning policy — proxy point may have no tuning policy assigned (defaults to never polling). Assign appropriate policy.
4. Verify Device Instance — confirm the proxy point is bound to the correct BACnet object on the correct PBC. After a discovery, it's possible to bind to the wrong object if multiple devices have similar device instances.
5. Verify IP address — if PBC IP changed (DHCP reassignment), the device in N4 may be pointing to an old IP. Re-discover or update the device's IP binding.
6. Verify the BACnet object type and instance — use Phoenix Controls Workbench to browse the PBC's BACnet objects and confirm the Object Type (AI, AV, AO) and Object Instance match what's configured in the N4 proxy point.

## FUME HOOD 4 HOODS MAXIMUM EXHAUST — SCHEDULE AND ZPS CAUSES
When multiple fume hoods go to maximum exhaust simultaneously at an unexpected time:
- **Schedule cause**: A WeeklySchedule or occupancy command in Niagara 4 or the BAS may have sent an Occupied command that triggered maximum exhaust. Check the schedule logic — verify the schedule output at the time of the event. An incorrectly configured schedule could send maximum exhaust during what appears to be off-hours.
- **ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) cause**: If ZPS sensors are wired and configured, a ZPS malfunction (stuck contact, wiring fault) could trigger an occupancy change that drives hoods to full exhaust. Check ZPS wiring and status in the Flow Manager App.
- **Wire sheet logic cause**: A kitControl wire sheet sequence may have written an emergency exhaust command at a priority that overrides normal operation. Check the BACnet Priority Array on the Emergency_Exhaust proxy point — if Priority 1 is active, something wrote to it. Check station logs: Config > Services > LogService for entries at the time of the event.
- **Setpoint write cause**: A BAS setpoint write or override may have commanded maximum CFM. Check the BACnet Priority Array on the exhaust flow setpoint proxy point to see what's active and at what priority level.
- Emergency exhaust active, fire alarm, or local button press remain the most likely causes — but always check schedule, ZPS, and wire sheet logic as secondary causes before declaring a hardware fault.

## CSCP COMMISSIONING — FIRST STEP IS MECHANICAL AND DUCT COMPLETION
Before commissioning any Phoenix Controls CSCP system: verify all mechanical installation is complete. This means:
- Ductwork installed, sealed, tested for leaks, and balanced (TAB complete or in progress by air balancing contractor)
- All venturi valve bodies physically installed in ductwork per design drawings — correct size, orientation, and upstream/downstream clearances
- Air handling units operational and delivering design static pressure (minimum 0.3" WC across low-pressure valves)
- All 24VAC power wiring complete and verified (±10% nominal at each device)
- All RS485 MS/TP signal wiring complete with termination resistors at bus ends
- Air balancing verification — without adequate duct static pressure, commissioning results are meaningless
Only after mechanical completion is confirmed do you proceed to power-up, IP discovery (arp -a), and Phoenix Controls Workbench commissioning.

## COMMISSIONING 30-ROOM STRATEGY — PRIORITY ORDER
When re-commissioning 30 rooms simultaneously on a tight schedule, the priority order is:
1. **Life safety rooms first** — any lab with BSL designation, fume hoods over hazardous materials, or negative pressure requirements. These must be commissioned and verified before occupancy.
2. **Safety verification as you go** — do not sign off any room as complete without running a face velocity verification test and confirming pressurization offset is within spec. Verification and documentation are non-negotiable even under schedule pressure.
3. **Parallel commissioning** — assign technicians to separate PBC groups (PBC-01 rooms 1–5, PBC-02 rooms 6–10, etc.). Independent PBC groups can be commissioned in parallel without interference.
4. **Use commissioning templates** — after first room in each room type is complete, copy configuration to remaining similar rooms. Batch Workbench tools speed deployment.
