Check the schedule logic — verify the schedule output at the time of the event. An incorrectly configured schedule could send maximum exhaust during what appears to be off-hours.

topic: Niagara 4 Stale Points, Poll Scheduler, and Force Read (Part 2)
content:
- **ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) cause**: If ZPS sensors are wired and configured, a ZPS malfunction (stuck contact, wiring fault) could trigger an occupancy change that drives hoods to full exhaust. Check ZPS wiring and status in the Flow Manager App.
- **Wire sheet logic cause**: A kitControl wire sheet sequence may have written an emergency exhaust command at a priority that overrides normal operation. Check the BACnet Priority Array on the Emergency_Exhaust proxy point — if Priority 1 is active, something wrote to it. Check station logs: Config > Services > LogService for entries at the time of the event.
- **Setpoint write cause**: A BAS setpoint write or override may have commanded maximum CFM. Check the BACnet Priority Array on the exhaust flow setpoint proxy point to see what's active and at what priority level.
- Emergency exhaust active, fire alarm, or local button press remain the most likely causes — but always check schedule, ZPS, and wire sheet logic as secondary causes before declaring a hardware fault.

## CSCP COMMISSIONING — FIRST STEP IS MECHANICAL AND DUCT COMPLETION
Before commissioning any Phoenix Controls CSCP system: verify all mechanical installation is complete. This means:
- Ductwork installed, sealed, tested for leaks, and balanced (TAB complete or in progress by air balancing contractor)
- All venturi valve bodies physically installed in ductwork per design drawings — correct size, orientation, and upstream/downstream clearances
- Air handling units operational and delivering design static pressure (minimum 0.3" WC across low-pressure valves)
- All 24VAC power wiring complete and verified (±10% nominal at each device)
- All RS485 MS/TP signal wiring complete with termination resistors at bus ends
- Air balancing verification — without adequate duct static pressure, commissioning results are meaningless
Only after mechanical completion is confirmed do you proceed to power-up, IP discovery (arp -a), and Phoenix Controls Workbench commissioning.

## COMMISSIONING 30-ROOM STRATEGY — PRIORITY ORDER
When re-commissioning 30 rooms simultaneously on a tight schedule, the priority order is:
1. **Life safety rooms first** — any lab with BSL designation, fume hoods over hazardous materials, or negative pressure requirements. These must be commissioned and verified before occupancy.
2. **Safety verification as you go** — do not sign off any room as complete without running a face velocity verification test and confirming pressurization offset is within spec. Verification and documentation are non-negotiable even under schedule pressure.
3. **Parallel commissioning** — assign technicians to separate PBC groups (PBC-01 rooms 1–5, PBC-02 rooms 6–10, etc.). Independent PBC groups can be commissioned in parallel without interference.
4. **Use commissioning templates** — after first room in each room type is complete, copy configuration to remaining similar rooms. Batch Workbench tools speed deployment.
5. **Life safety documentation** — maintain a commissioning log showing date, technician, face velocity measurement, and pressurization result for each room. This is required for occupancy sign-off.

## BSL-3 COMMISSIONING — VERIFY AND OFFSET
For BSL-3 lab commissioning with Phoenix Controls:
- **Verify** pressurization at every boundary — each room boundary pressure differential must be independently verified against the design specification, not assumed
- **Offset verification** — volumetric offset (exhaust CFM minus supply CFM) must be confirmed at each operating condition: hood sash open, sash closed, occupied, unoccupied. Offset must be maintained in all conditions.
- Minimum negative pressure: -0.05" WC measured at room boundary relative to corridor
- All exhaust valves must be set to Fail-Open (FSM configured for fail-open) — on power loss, exhaust stays open to prevent containment failure
- All supply valves must be set to Fail-Closed on power loss to prevent unfiltered air ingress
- Redundant exhaust fans must be tested for automatic switchover — verify both fans, verify switchover timing
- All alarms must be tested and routing confirmed before occupancy

## I/P TRANSDUCER — 4–20mA INPUT, 3–15 PSI OUTPUT
The I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer on pneumatic Phoenix Controls valve systems:
- Input signal: 4–20mA DC current loop from controller (MIJ or PBC)
- Output: 3–15 PSI pneumatic air pressure to actuator
- 4mA = 3 PSI (minimum — valve at minimum/closed position)
- 20mA = 15 PSI (maximum — valve at full open position)
- Linear relationship between 4–20mA and 3–15 PSI
- Supply pressure to I/P must be at least 20 PSI minimum (preferably 25–30 PSI regulated) for full output range
- If I/P supply pressure drops below 15 PSI, the transducer cannot deliver 15 PSI output even at 20mA signal — valve won't fully open
- Common field check: measure current at I/P input terminals (should read 4mA at minimum, 20mA at maximum), then measure pneumatic output (should read 3 PSI at minimum, 15 PSI at maximum)

## PNEUMATIC VALVE SLOW RESPONSE — ORIFICE AND I/P CAUSES
When a pneumatic Celeris valve takes 45 seconds to stroke instead of 8 seconds:
- **Orifice restriction** — the I/P transducer has an internal orifice that controls airflow rate to the actuator. A partially clogged orifice dramatically reduces stroking speed. This is often caused by moisture or particulate in the instrument air supply. Check the instrument air supply filter/dryer. If orifice is clogged, the I/P transducer must be replaced — the orifice is not field-cleanable.
- **I/P transducer failure** — a degraded I/P transducer may be outputting the correct pressure at static, but have insufficient flow capacity to stroke the actuator quickly. Replace the I/P transducer and observe stroke speed.
- Also check: tubing length (max 75 ft), tubing restrictions, air supply pressure (min 15 PSI), actuator condition, and any inline restrictions in pneumatic circuit.

## PRESSURE DIFFERENTIAL DRIFT — VENTURI DIRTY AND AGING
When a Phoenix Controls system passes commissioning but pressure differential readings become inconsistent over months:
- **Dirty venturi** — the venturi valve body's flow measurement depends on the pressure differential across the venturi throat. Dust, particulate, or contamination buildup inside the valve body changes the effective throat area and causes flow measurement drift. Inspect and clean the venturi interior annually in dusty environments.
- **Aging pressure transducer** — the differential pressure transducer (if installed) drifts with age, thermal cycling, and humidity. Replace transducers that are 3+ years old in humid or chemically corrosive environments. Use a calibrated reference manometer to verify transducer accuracy annually.
- **Sensor aging** — all electronic sensors drift over time. A system that was accurate at commissioning may drift ±5–10% over 3–5 years without recalibration.

## BACNET COV vs POLLING — BANDWIDTH AND TUNING POLICY
COV (Change of Value) subscriptions vs polled reading for Phoenix Controls points in Niagara 4:
- **COV (event-driven)**: The PBC sends an update only when the value changes by more than a configured threshold (COV_Increment). No polling — device pushes updates. Benefits: dramatically lower network bandwidth (only sends when value changes, not every second), lower JACE CPU load (no poll scheduling overhead), faster notification of alarm state changes.
- **Polling (time-driven)**: JACE requests the current value at a fixed interval (Fast/Normal/Slow bucket rate) regardless of whether it changed. Benefits: guaranteed maximum age, works even if COV subscription fails.
- **Tuning policy** controls both: the poll bucket rate AND the COV subscription settings (COV enabled, COV lifetime, COV increment). Every Phoenix proxy point should have a tuning policy assigned — without one, Niagara defaults to no polling and the point goes stale immediately.
- Bandwidth impact: on a large Phoenix install (200+ proxy points), using polling at 5s intervals generates thousands of BACnet requests per minute. Using COV for stable points (alarm states, setpoints) reduces this to hundreds of requests — a 90%+ reduction in BACnet traffic. This is why COV + polling backup is the recommended approach for large sites.
- Phoenix Controls alarm points (Flow_Alarm, FV_Low_Alarm, Emergency_Active): ALWAYS use COV. These change infrequently but must arrive immediately. COV with a 5-second poll backup (belt-and-suspenders) is the correct configuration.

## HIGH-SIGNAL-SELECT — HIGH SIGNAL KEYWORD
The MIJ high-signal-select strategy: the supply valve receives the HIGH SIGNAL — the higher of two competing demand signals. The term "high signal select" (sometimes written as high-signal-select or high signal selector) refers to always choosing the maximum (highest-value) demand signal among multiple inputs. In Phoenix Controls systems: the supply air CFM command = the HIGHER of (thermal demand CFM, lab pressurization required CFM). This ensures both temperature control and pressurization are always satisfied — whichever requires more supply air wins. In Niagara 4 kitControl: the HighSignalSelect component (under kitControl > Selects > HighSignalSelect) implements this logic directly with two or more numeric inputs and one output.

## N4 FOLDER STRUCTURE — ORD PATHS
In Niagara 4, every object has an ORD (Object Resolution Descriptor) path — the unique address used to locate it in the station hierarchy. ORDs are used in Px graphics bindings, alarm routing, history naming, and inter-station links. For Phoenix Controls systems, consistent ORD naming is critical:
- ORDs follow the pattern: station:|slot:/Config/Drivers/BacnetNetwork/[DeviceName]/points/[PointName]
- For history: history:|/HistoryService/[HistoryId]
- For alarms: alarm:|/AlarmService/AlarmClasses/[ClassName]
- Name points using consistent convention: Building_Floor_Room_Device_PointType (no spaces, use underscores)
- Example ORD: station:|slot:/Config/Drivers/BacnetNetwork/PBC_Zone01/points/Zone_Exhaust_CFM
- Px graphics bind to ORDs — a renamed point breaks the Px binding. Plan naming convention before adding any proxy points to avoid future graphic rebuilding.

## EMERGENCY EXHAUST — VERIFIED AND MONITORED STATUS
For Phoenix Controls emergency exhaust systems:
- **Verified** status means the exhaust valve has physically reached (or is confirmed to be at) maximum position. On CSCP systems, verified status comes from the ACM position feedback (Vpot reading at maximum). The system is considered "verified" only when actual valve position matches commanded position.
- **Monitored** emergency exhaust means the condition is being continuously checked — the BMS (N4 or BAS) has a proxy point tracking the Emergency_Active status from the PBC. Any loss of the monitoring signal itself (stale proxy point) should generate an alarm — you cannot monitor safety equipment with a stale status.
- **Emergency exhaust active** means at least one of these has triggered: fire alarm dry contact, manual emergency button, BACnet command at Priority 1–3, or PBC internal safety logic
- In BSL-3 and critical applications: verified AND monitored means the system both confirms the valve reached full open AND continuously watches that it stays there. If the valve fails to verify (position doesn't match command), that itself is an alarm condition.

topic: Commissioning Prerequisites, 30-Room Strategy, and BSL-3 (Part 1)

- All ductwork must be installed, sealed, and balanced. The AHU must be operational and delivering design static pressure.
- All venturi valve bodies must be physically installed, bolted, and sealed into the ductwork. No loose connections or missing gaskets.
- Air balancing (TAB) must be complete or at least verified — the system needs to deliver the design static pressure range (0.3"–3.0" WC for low-pressure valves, minimum 0.3" WC). Without adequate duct static pressure, the valves cannot achieve accurate flow, and commissioning results will be invalid.
- All power wiring must be complete: 24VAC at each device terminal, within ±10% nominal.
- All signal/control wiring must be complete: RS485 MS/TP for CSCP, LonWorks for Celeris 2, N2 bus for PHX/MIJ systems.
- Only after confirming mechanical completion and duct static pressure is verified do you proceed with device discovery, parameter configuration, and commissioning.
- Hardware first, software second — a wiring fault discovered during physical inspection saves hours versus discovering it during a failed commissioning attempt.

## NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER SETUP — COMPLETE STEP-BY-STEP
Setting up the BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 for Phoenix Controls PBCs requires these specific steps:
1. **Open Phoenix Controls Workbench** (N4-based, version 3.0+) and connect to the JACE platform via Fox protocol port 1911
2. **Install BACnet module**: Platform > Software Manager — verify bacnet-rt.jar is installed. If missing, install and restart.
3. **Add BacnetNetwork**: Nav tree > Config > Drivers > right-click > New > BacnetNetwork from bacnet palette
4. **Configure BacnetNetwork**:
   - Set Local Device Object ID (unique BACnet device instance for this JACE — e.g. 9001)
   - Set Device Name (descriptive)
5. **Configure BacnetCommLinkIp** (for BACnet/IP):
   - Expand BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort
   - **Enabled: must be set to TRUE** — this defaults to FALSE and is the #1 most commonly missed step
   - Local IP Address: set to the JACE's actual IP (not 0.0.0.0)
   - UDP Port: 47808 (do not change)
   - Network Number: unique number for this subnet
6. **Configure BacnetCommLinkMstp** (if PBCs are on RS485 trunk):
   - BacnetNetwork > Add > BacnetCommLinkMstp
   - Set COM port, baud rate (76800 for CSCP), Max Master: 127, Enabled: TRUE
7. **Enable BacnetNetwork**: right-click > Actions > Enable. Status should go green.
8. **Discover PBCs**: right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover (or Views > Device Manager > Discover)
9. **Add discovered PBCs** to database by double-clicking or selecting Add
10. **Discover proxy points**: right-click PBC device > Views > Points > Discover — all BACnet objects enumerated
11. **Add proxy points**: select desired objects > Subscribe/Add — points appear in station
12. **Assign tuning policies**: Fast for alarms and safety points, Normal for CFM actuals, Slow for setpoints/energy
13. **Verify poll scheduler**: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Poll Scheduler — keep busy % below 75%
Key terms used during this process: BacnetNetwork, Drivers, Discover, Device Instance, Enabled, UDP, 47808, proxy point, tuning policy

## NIAGARA 4 PBC NOT APPEARING IN DEVICE DISCOVERY — ALL SIX CAUSES
When a newly installed PBC does not show up in Niagara 4 BACnet device discovery:
1. **PBC has no valid IP address** — check power, boot LED, then run arp -a on Windows ~15s after power-up. If no IP: DHCP server issue or network cable not connected.
2. **BacnetNetwork not enabled or IpPort Enabled=false** — the most commonly missed step. BacnetNetwork > BacnetComm > Network > IpPort > Enabled MUST be TRUE. Also verify BacnetNetwork itself is enabled (right-click > Actions > Enable).
3. **BBMD not configured** — if JACE and PBC are on different VLANs/subnets, BACnet UDP broadcasts won't cross without a BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device). Configure BBMD on the BacnetCommLinkIp with a BDT entry for the PBC's subnet.
4. **Duplicate Device Instance** — if another device on the network has the same BACnet device instance as the new PBC, discovery collisions occur. Check all device instances are unique. Configure PBC device instance in Phoenix Controls Workbench (PBC-CT) before connecting to N4.
5. **Wrong baud rate on MS/TP** — for MS/TP-connected PBCs, the MstpPort baud rate in N4 must match the PBC's configured baud rate. Verify both are set to 76800 (or whatever rate was set during PBC commissioning).

In Niagara 4, every object has an ORD (Object Resolution Descriptor) path — the unique address used to locate it in the station hierarchy. ORDs are used in Px graphics bindings, alarm routing, history naming, and inter-station links. For Phoenix Controls systems, consistent ORD naming is critical:
- ORDs follow the pattern: station:|slot:/Config/Drivers/BacnetNetwork/[DeviceName]/points/[PointName]
- For history: history:|/HistoryService/[HistoryId]
- For alarms: alarm:|/AlarmService/AlarmClasses/[ClassName]
- Name points using consistent convention: Building_Floor_Room_Device_PointType (no spaces, use underscores)
- Example ORD: station:|slot:/Config/Drivers/BacnetNetwork/PBC_Zone01/points/Zone_Exhaust_CFM
- Px graphics bind to ORDs — a renamed point breaks the Px binding. Plan naming convention before adding any proxy points to avoid future graphic rebuilding.

## EMERGENCY EXHAUST — VERIFIED AND MONITORED STATUS
For Phoenix Controls emergency exhaust systems:
- **Verified** status means the exhaust valve has physically reached (or is confirmed to be at) maximum position. On CSCP systems, verified status comes from the ACM position feedback (Vpot reading at maximum). The system is considered "verified" only when actual valve position matches commanded position.
- **Monitored** emergency exhaust means the condition is being continuously checked — the BMS (N4 or BAS) has a proxy point tracking the Emergency_Active status from the PBC. Any loss of the monitoring signal itself (stale proxy point) should generate an alarm — you cannot monitor safety equipment with a stale status.
- **Emergency exhaust active** means at least one of these has triggered: fire alarm dry contact, manual emergency button, BACnet command at Priority 1–3, or PBC internal safety logic
- In BSL-3 and critical applications: verified AND monitored means the system both confirms the valve reached full open AND continuously watches that it stays there. If the valve fails to verify (position doesn't match command), that itself is an alarm condition.

HIGH-VALUE ADDITIONS — FIELD-CRITICAL KNOWLEDGE
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

topic: BACnet COV vs Polling, High-Signal-Select, ORD Paths, Emergency Exhaust Monitoring
content:
## BACNET COV vs POLLING — BANDWIDTH AND TUNING POLICY
COV (Change of Value) subscriptions vs polled reading for Phoenix Controls points in Niagara 4:
- **COV (event-driven)**: The PBC sends an update only when the value changes by more than a configured threshold (COV_Increment). No polling — device pushes updates. Benefits: dramatically lower network bandwidth (only sends when value changes, not every second), lower JACE CPU load (no poll scheduling overhead), faster notification of alarm state changes.
- **Polling (time-driven)**: JACE requests the current value at a fixed interval (Fast/Normal/Slow bucket rate) regardless of whether it changed. Benefits: guaranteed maximum age, works even if COV subscription fails.
- **Tuning policy** controls both: the poll bucket rate AND the COV subscription settings (COV enabled, COV lifetime, COV increment). Every Phoenix proxy point should have a tuning policy assigned — without one, Niagara defaults to no polling and the point goes stale immediately.
- Bandwidth impact: on a large Phoenix install (200+ proxy points), using polling at 5s intervals generates thousands of BACnet requests per minute. Using COV for stable points (alarm states, setpoints) reduces this to hundreds of requests — a 90%+ reduction in BACnet traffic. This is why COV + polling backup is the recommended approach for large sites.
- Phoenix Controls alarm points (Flow_Alarm, FV_Low_Alarm, Emergency_Active): ALWAYS use COV. These change infrequently but must arrive immediately. COV with a 5-second poll backup (belt-and-suspenders) is the correct configuration.

## HIGH-SIGNAL-SELECT — HIGH SIGNAL KEYWORD
The MIJ high-signal-select strategy: the supply valve receives the HIGH SIGNAL — the higher of two competing demand signals. The term "high signal select" (sometimes written as high-signal-select or high signal selector) refers to always choosing the maximum (highest-value) demand signal among multiple inputs. In Phoenix Controls systems: the supply air CFM command = the HIGHER of (thermal demand CFM, lab pressurization required CFM). This ensures both temperature control and pressurization are always satisfied — whichever requires more supply air wins. In Niagara 4 kitControl: the HighSignalSelect component (under kitControl > Selects > HighSignalSelect) implements this logic directly with two or more numeric inputs and one output.

topic: FHM430/530/631 Complete 23-Parameter Calibration Reference
content:
## FHM430 / FHM530 / FHM631 — COMPLETE 23-PARAMETER REFERENCE WITH VALID RANGES

This is the complete calibration parameter table for X30 series FHM boards. Parameter 1 MUST be set first — it defines all subsequent parameter behavior. Use the Room Schedule Sheet (RSS) for site-specific values.

| # | Parameter Name | FHM430 | FHM530 | FHM631 | Valid Range / Notes |
|---|----------------|--------|--------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | FHM Mode | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | vAvv, vAvd, cvn, cvA, cvS, cvSA, 2St, 2StS, 2StA — SET FIRST |
| 2 | Normal face velocity setpoint | ✓ | — | ✓ | 60–200 fpm. Typical: 100 fpm |
| 3 | Setback face velocity setpoint | ✓ | — | ✓ | 40–150 fpm. Typical: 60–80 fpm. Must be < Parameter 2 |
| 4 | Face velocity alarm low setpoint | ✓ | — | ✓ | 40–150 fpm. Typical: 80 fpm. Must be < Parameter 2 |
| 5 | Face velocity alarm high setpoint | ✓ | — | ✓ | 80–250 fpm. Typical: 150 fpm. Must be > Parameter 2 |
| 6 | Sash width | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 1–99 inches. Measure interior opening width |
| 7 | Sash maximum open height | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 1–60 inches. Measure maximum usable sash opening height |
| 8 | Maximum hood exhaust flow | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 50–9999 CFM. From valve nameplate or engineering spec |
| 9 | Minimum sash opening (alarm threshold) | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0–60 inches. Alarm fires if sash opens beyond this. Typically max sash height |
| 10 | Sash sensor — fully open voltage/resistance | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Capture live reading with sash fully open. Store exactly. |
| 11 | Sash sensor — fully closed voltage/resistance | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Capture live reading with sash fully closed. Store exactly. |
| 12 | Minimum supply flow | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0–9999 CFM. Minimum supply air when hood is in setback. From engineering spec. |
| 13 | Minimum setback exhaust clamp | ✓ | — | ✓ | 50–9999 CFM. Minimum exhaust flow even in setback. Typically 10% of max. |
| 14 | Alarm delay | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 0–60 seconds. Delay before face velocity alarm activates. Typical: 4–10s |
| 15 | Sash switch point (FHM530) / Broken sash threshold (FHM430/631) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | FHM530: CFM at which two-state valve switches. FHM430/631: % of max sash where broken sash alarm fires |
| 16 | Sash fully closed threshold | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Voltage/resistance value below which sash is considered fully closed. Typically 10% of P11 value |
| 17 | Broken sash threshold | ✓ | — | ✓ | Voltage/resistance above which sash sensor is considered broken/disconnected |
| 18 | Emergency mutable | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Y = emergency alarm can be muted by operator. N = cannot mute. Life safety rooms: N |
| 19 | Beeper volume | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 0–3 (0=off, 1=low, 2=medium, 3=max) |
| 20 | Auto mute | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Y = alarm auto-mutes after mute duration. N = must manually mute |
| 21 | Mute duration | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 1–60 minutes. Time before muted alarm re-activates |
| 22 | Energy waste alert (FHM631 only) | — | — | ✓ | Y/N. Enables sash-open + dark-room alert |
| 23 | Decommission mode (FHM631 only) | — | — | ✓ | Y/N. Enables hibernation via faceplate button |

**Entering calibration mode**: Press Mute + Emergency Exhaust simultaneously for 2 seconds. Two beeps confirm entry. Navigate with faceplate buttons. Save each parameter before advancing.

**Critical calibration pitfalls:**
- Parameters 10 and 11 (sash sensor calibration) must be captured with sash physically at the exact position — do not estimate. One tech holds the sash, the other captures the reading.
- Parameter 8 (max exhaust) must match the valve's physical flow range or the board will command flows the valve cannot achieve, causing chronic flow alarms.
- Parameter 2 and 3 (face velocity setpoints) must be achievable by the valve at the installed duct static pressure. Verify duct static is ≥0.3" WC before commissioning.
- After changing Parameter 1, the board may reset other parameters to defaults — always re-verify all parameters after a mode change.

---

## CELERIS 1 — ACCEL-WORKS COMMISSIONING PROCEDURE

Celeris 1 uses LonWorks with LNS (LonWorks Network Services) architecture. Commissioning requires Accel-Works software — NOT Phoenix Controls Workbench and NOT LonMaker (which is for Celeris 2).

**Required equipment:**
- Laptop with Accel-Works installed (Windows XP or Windows 7 compatible — may require VM on modern systems)
- Optical isolator (LON USB adapter) — typically Echelon U10/U20 or equivalent
- Network interface cable from optical isolator to LON trunk

**Pre-commissioning checklist:**
- Verify all Celeris 1 LVC boards are powered (24VAC at TB4)
- Verify LON trunk wiring is daisy-chained (not star) with correct termination
- Verify each LVC has a unique physical node address set via SW1 DIP switches
- Back up any existing Accel-Works database before making changes — databases are not recoverable without backup

**Commissioning procedure:**
1. Connect optical isolator to laptop USB and LON trunk
2. Open Accel-Works → File → Open Project (or New Project for fresh installation)
3. Network Manager → Discover → Accel-Works sends Service Pin request to all devices
4. Each LVC on the trunk responds — appears in discovered device list with its Neuron ID
5. For each discovered LVC:
   a. Right-click → Commission → assigns the LVC's Neuron ID to its database entry
   b. Right-click → Download → pushes application code and configuration to LVC
   c. Set node address (must match SW1 DIP setting on board)
   d. Configure flow parameters: min CFM, max CFM, fail-safe position
   e. Set baud rate (LON TP/FT-10 at 78.125 kbps — fixed, not adjustable)
6. Bind functional blocks: Network Manager → Bind → connect LVC outputs (flow command) to FHM inputs (flow feedback) and to zone controller
7. After binding: verify each valve responds to commands from Accel-Works
8. Save and export database — keep multiple backups in different locations

**Critical notes on Celeris 1:**
- **Never** use LonMaker on a Celeris 1 network — LonMaker uses a different binding architecture and will corrupt the Accel-Works database
- **Database backup is non-negotiable** — if an LVC fails and the Accel-Works database is lost, the replacement LVC cannot be re-commissioned without the original Neuron ID mapping
- The Celeris 1 PEM card provides ±15VDC power to FHM boards — when upgrading Celeris 1 LVCs to Celeris 2, the PEM card is removed and a separate 24VAC transformer must be added for FHM power
- Accel-Link cable (existing Celeris 1 trunk cable) CAN be reused for LON communications when upgrading to Celeris 2, but the shield wire must be isolated and not connected to any Celeris 2 device

---

## MIJ400 / MIJ500 — COMPLETE HVAC PRO COMMISSIONING PROCEDURE

The MIJ (Makeup Air Interface) requires HVAC PRO for Windows Rev 4.0 or later. Only factory-certified Phoenix Controls technicians may commission MIJ systems. This procedure is for reference and verification.

**Required equipment:**
- Laptop with HVAC PRO for Windows Rev 4.0+ installed
- AS-CBLPRO-1 or AS-CBLPRO-2 cable (Zone Bus cable — 8-pin phone jack connector)
- Digital voltmeter

**Pre-commissioning checklist:**
- Verify ±15VDC power supply at MIJ TB4: Pin 1 = +15VDC ±5%, Pin 3 = -15VDC ±5%
- If voltage is out of spec: locate power supply board (remove top metal plate of MIJ enclosure), adjust potentiometer for + and − rail independently
- Verify all FHI boards have correct N2 bus addresses set via DIP switches (unique, 1–32)
- Verify all SW1/SW2 analog input switches on FHI boards match the sensor types wired
- Verify N2 bus end-of-line termination at both physical ends of N2 bus

**Commissioning procedure:**
1. Connect AS-CBLPRO cable between laptop and MIJ Zone Bus port (8-pin phone jack)
2. Open HVAC PRO → select Phoenix Lab and Fume Hood Interface application
3. File → Open (or New for fresh installation)
4. For each fume hood, configure:
   - Hood Max Flow (CFM) — from valve nameplate
   - Hood Min Flow (CFM) — from engineering spec
   - Max Sash Area (ft²) — sash width × max open height ÷ 144
   - Min Sash Area (ft²) — minimum functional sash opening area
   - Normal Face Velocity Setpoint (fpm) — typically 100 fpm
   - Setback Face Velocity Setpoint (fpm) — typically 60–80 fpm
5. Configure temperature control:
   - Occupied Heating Setpoint (°F) — typically 70°F
   - Occupied Cooling Setpoint (°F) — typically 76°F
   - Unoccupied Heating Setpoint (°F) — typically 60°F
   - Unoccupied Cooling Setpoint (°F) — typically 85°F
   - Cooling Proportional Band (°F) — typically 4°F
   - Heating Proportional Band (°F) — typically 4°F
   - Cooling Integral Gain — typically 0.02
   - Heating Integral Gain — typically 0.02
6. Set N2 Bus address for each FHI (must match hardware DIP switch setting)
7. Database → Download to Controller → confirm download complete
8. Verify N2 Comm Status and ZB Comm Status data points show OK
9. Test each hood: open sash, verify CFM command tracks face velocity setpoint × sash area
10. Test emergency exhaust: trigger via FHM faceplate, verify MIJ commands all hoods to maximum
11. Test pressure alarm: verify DP switch triggers alarm at <0.6" WC threshold

**MIJ troubleshooting reference:**
| Symptom | Check |
|---------|-------|
| Zone Bus comm error | Cycle power on PHX. Check 8-pin phone jack seating. |
| N2 Bus comm error | Verify N2 address unique. Check EOL termination. Verify N2 cable not in power conduit. |
| ±15VDC out of spec | Remove MIJ top plate. Adjust power supply potentiometer (+ and − independently). |
| EXP board LED abnormal | Check 40-pin ribbon cable between MIJ → EXP → FHI. Reseat all connectors. |
| Wrong thermal demand CFM | Verify FHI SW1/SW2 analog input switches for temperature sensor AI. Recalibrate if needed. |
| Pressure alarm constant | Check DP switch tubing — blocked or kinked is most common. Verify AHU operating. |

---

## VINTAGE PNEUMATIC VALVES — COMPLETE FIELD REFERENCE

### Vintage Identification (critical before any work)
| Vintage | Base Type | Valve Shape | PCB Connector | Pivot Arm Hole | Actuator per Dual |
|---------|-----------|-------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|
| A/B | U-channel | Accel I hourglass | Telco | 3/16" | Two |
| C | Flat | Accel II diffused | Telco | 3/16" | Two |
| D/E | Flat | Accel II diffused | Terminal block | 1/4" | One |

### Pivot Arm Alignment — Most Common Cause of Wrong Flow
The pivot arm connects the actuator to the valve cone. Incorrect alignment is the #1 cause of wrong flow on pneumatic CV and two-state valves after any maintenance work.

**Alignment procedure for CV valves:**
1. With air supply isolated and actuator fully stroked to minimum position, the valve cone should be at minimum flow position
2. Measure actual flow with a calibrated airstation or flow hood
3. If flow is wrong: loosen pivot arm set screw, reposition arm on actuator shaft, retighten
4. Re-verify flow at minimum AND maximum command
5. Vintage A/B/C: arm fits 3/16" shaft. Vintage D/E: arm fits 1/4" shaft — do not swap between vintages

**Alignment procedure for two-state valves:**
1. Verify pneumatic supply pressure at controller inlet (15–20 PSI)
2. With minimum signal (4mA / 3 PSI): valve should be at closed (or minimum) position
3. With maximum signal (20mA / 15 PSI): valve should be at full open position
4. If valve doesn't reach either position correctly: check pressure supply first, then recheck pivot arm alignment
5. Two-state valves have a fixed mechanical stop — do not force beyond stop

### Pressure Switch (PSL) — Replacement and Orientation
The PSL pressure switch monitors duct static pressure across the valve. If pressure drops below 0.6" WC, it opens and triggers a pressure alarm.

**Critical on replacement**: The pressure switch hose orientation MUST replicate the original exactly.
- High-pressure port connects to upstream (supply) side of valve
- Low-pressure port connects to downstream side
- Reversed hose connections = inverted pressure measurement = alarm at normal operation, no alarm at low pressure (dangerous)
- Before removing old switch: photograph the hose connections and label each hose H (high) and L (low)
- Blocked or kinked pressure switch tubing is the most common cause of a pressure alarm when AHU is running normally — check tubing before suspecting the switch

### Pneumatic System Commissioning Sequence
1. **Verify instrument air supply**: 15–20 PSI at regulator outlet, clean and dry (dew point < 35°F). Moisture = sluggish response, corrosion, I/P failure.
2. **Leak test**: Pressurize system to 20 PSI with nitrogen (NOT compressed air — moisture risk). Isolate source. Monitor for 10 minutes — pressure drop > 0.5 PSI = unacceptable. Apply soapy water to all fittings.
3. **I/P calibration check**: Apply 4mA signal → verify 3 PSI output. Apply 20mA → verify 15 PSI output. If out of spec, adjust I/P span and zero (small adjustment screws on I/P body). Replace I/P if adjustment cannot correct.
4. **Actuator stroke verification**: Stroke valve from minimum to maximum signal, verify smooth travel throughout range, no binding, correct end positions.
5. **Pivot arm alignment verification**: Verify flow at minimum and maximum positions against design spec.
6. **Pressure switch verification**: Reduce duct static below 0.6" WC (close upstream damper), verify alarm triggers at FHM display. Restore pressure, verify alarm clears.
7. **Face velocity verification**: Open sash to design position, verify face velocity meets spec with a handheld anemometer.

---

1. **Verify instrument air supply**: 15–20 PSI at regulator outlet, clean and dry (dew point < 35°F). Moisture = sluggish response, corrosion, I/P failure.
2. **Leak test**: Pressurize system to 20 PSI with nitrogen (NOT compressed air — moisture risk). Isolate source. Monitor for 10 minutes — pressure drop > 0.5 PSI = unacceptable. Apply soapy water to all fittings.
3. **I/P calibration check**: Apply 4mA signal → verify 3 PSI output. Apply 20mA → verify 15 PSI output. If out of spec, adjust I/P span and zero (small adjustment screws on I/P body). Replace I/P if adjustment cannot correct.
4. **Actuator stroke verification**: Stroke valve from minimum to maximum signal, verify smooth travel throughout range, no binding, correct end positions.
5. **Pivot arm alignment verification**: Verify flow at minimum and maximum positions against design spec.
6. **Pressure switch verification**: Reduce duct static below 0.6" WC (close upstream damper), verify alarm triggers at FHM display. Restore pressure, verify alarm clears.
7. **Face velocity verification**: Open sash to design position, verify face velocity meets spec with a handheld anemometer.

---

## SERVICE BULLETIN AWARENESS — KEY FIELD CHANGES

These are known procedural changes from Phoenix Controls that affect field work. Always verify current procedure with Phoenix Controls at (800) 340-0007 before performing on critical systems.

### CSCP — ACM Capacitor Discharge Before Board Removal
- **Issue**: Early CSCP ACM installations did not always document the FSM capacitor discharge requirement
- **Current requirement**: If ACM has FSM installed, capacitors must be fully discharged before removing the ACM board. Move J1 jumper alternately between N.O. and N.C. until actuator no longer moves. Do not skip this step.
- **Field indicator**: If you hear a sudden mechanical stroke when removing an ACM board, the capacitors were not discharged. Inspect actuator and downstream ductwork immediately.

### FHD500 — Baud Rate Mismatch After Firmware Update
- **Issue**: Some FHD500 firmware updates reset the baud rate to factory default (9600 bps) even if previously configured to 76800 bps
- **Symptom**: FHD500 shows Unlinked immediately after firmware update despite being properly wired
- **Fix**: Re-enter FHD500 Setup Wizard → Communication Settings step → verify and re-set baud rate to match trunk. Save.

### Celeris 2 — PEM Card Power When Upgrading from Celeris 1
- **Issue**: Technicians upgrading Celeris 1 to Celeris 2 sometimes remove the Celeris 1 PEM card without providing an alternate ±15VDC source for the FHM boards
- **Result**: All FHM boards lose power when PEM card removed. If discovered mid-project, hoods are uncontrolled.
- **Requirement**: Before removing any Celeris 1 PEM card, install a separate 24VAC transformer and verify FHM boards receive correct power from new source.

### X30 FHM — Parameter 1 After Factory Reset
- **Issue**: A factory reset (holding specific button combination during power-up) clears ALL parameters including Parameter 1
- **Result**: Board reverts to default mode (vAvd on FHM631) which may not match application
- **Fix**: Always re-set Parameter 1 first after any factory reset before recalibrating

### MIJ400/500 — Scale Factor Verification on Replacement
- **Issue**: Replacement MIJ boards ordered as "standard" may have different thermal demand scale factors than the original
- **Result**: Supply valve commands wrong CFM relative to thermal demand — room temperature control is off or oscillating
- **Fix**: When ordering replacement MIJ, provide original MIJ serial number to Phoenix Controls so they can match the scale factor configuration

---

topic: Failure Mode Library — Field-Verified Causes and Fixes
content:
## FAILURE MODE LIBRARY — FIELD-VERIFIED CAUSES AND FIXES

This section documents real failure patterns encountered in Phoenix Controls field service. Use this when textbook diagnostics don't match what you're seeing.

### "New board commissioned correctly but flow alarm fires immediately"
**Cause 90% of the time**: Parameter 1 wrong mode. The board thinks it's a different application type. A VAV board (vAvv) installed on a CV application will alarm because it expects sash feedback it isn't getting. A CV board (cvn) on a VAV application will never track sash position.
**Fix**: Re-enter calibration. Set Parameter 1 first. Re-calibrate all parameters.

### "Valve tracks correctly at mid-range but wrong at extremes"
**Cause**: Parameters 10 and 11 (sash sensor calibration) captured at wrong sash positions, OR sash physically doesn't reach fully open/closed during calibration.
**Fix**: Verify sash physically reaches end stops. Re-capture Parameters 10 and 11 with sash held firmly at each extreme. The delta between P10 and P11 should span the full signal range — if it doesn't, the sensor or cable has a problem.

### "System commissioned and working, then fails 2 weeks later with Unlinked"
**Cause**: MS/TP termination resistor installed incorrectly (middle of trunk, not at physical end) — system works initially because impedance is borderline, then fails as temperature or humidity changes shift cable impedance.
**Fix**: Walk the entire MS/TP trunk physically. Remove any termination not at the physical ends. Verify 120Ω at each true end with cable disconnected from devices.

### "Face velocity correct at setpoint but drifts low over the day"
**Cause**: Duct static pressure dropping as AHU filters load up (increasing restriction). The valve cannot maintain flow below minimum duct static.
**Fix**: Check duct static at beginning and end of day. If it's dropping below 0.3" WC, AHU filters need service. Coordinate with facilities. This is not a valve or controller problem.

### "ACM just replaced, now two other ACMs are offline"
**Cause**: Replacement ACM installed with same MAC address as an existing ACM (factory default or tech error). Three-way MAC conflict on trunk.
**Fix**: Power down the replacement ACM. Verify its SW1 DIP address in the off state. Set to a unique address. Reconnect. Original ACMs should recover within 30 seconds.

### "FHM shows correct CFM but BMS shows 0"
**Cause**: BMS proxy point is bound to the wrong BACnet object, or proxy point Enabled = false in Niagara, or device instance mismatch after board replacement (new board has default device instance, not the original).
**Fix**: Verify BACnet device instance on replacement board matches original (set in Workbench). Verify Niagara proxy point Enabled = true. Force Read on proxy point to confirm communication.

### "Celeris valve hunts but only when sash is between 30–70% open"
**Cause**: Sash sensor has a dead zone or nonlinearity in mid-range — loose wiring, damaged potentiometer track, or partial connector corrosion causing signal dropout in the middle of travel.
**Fix**: Monitor sash sensor voltage or resistance while slowly moving sash through full range. Look for any discontinuity or jump. Replace sensor if non-linear.

### "Pneumatic valve works fine cold but hunts when building warms up"
**Cause**: Pneumatic tubing has a small kink that becomes more restrictive as it warms (tubing softens and kink tightens). Or: I/P transducer orifice partially clogged — restricts flow at low signal when thermal expansion changes orifice geometry.
**Fix**: Inspect all tubing for kinks, especially near support brackets where tubing can press against metal. Replace any kinked section. Test I/P transducer by bypassing it temporarily with a known-good unit.

### "N4 poll scheduler was fine for months, now suddenly at 85%"
**Cause**: An IT network change (VLAN segmentation, switch replacement, firewall rule addition) broke COV subscriptions. All previously COV-subscribed points reverted to polling, multiplying poll load 3–5x.
**Fix**: Restart BacnetNetwork in N4 (disable/enable). This re-establishes all COV subscriptions. If scheduler stays high, check BBMD configuration — VLAN change may have broken broadcast forwarding.

topic: PBC Datasheet — Overview, Applications, LED Status (MKT-0511 Rev.C 11/2024)
content:
PROGRAMMABLE BACNET CONTROLLER (PBC) — MKT-0511 Rev.C 11/2024

The PBC can be mounted on a Phoenix Controls Venturi Valve or in a control panel and serves as the zone controller for offset control, comfort control, emergency control, dynamic pressurization control, and additional programmability.

FUNCTIONS:
- GEN (Generic Application) — standalone programmable logic and I/O module
- ZBH (Zone Balance High-Speed) — zone controller for high-speed valves (<1 sec response); handles up to 20 ACMs and 10 FHDs
- ZBL (Zone Balance Standard) — zone controller for up to four standard speed valves (<40–90 sec response)

SERIES:
- PBC 500 = BACnet MS/TP with BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)
- PBC 501 = BACnet MS/TP without BLE
- PBC 504 = BACnet IP with BLE
- PBC 505 = BACnet IP without BLE

PLATFORM: Crossover processor NXP I.MRT Cortex M7; 16 MB SDRAM; 16 MB QSPI Flash; Real-time clock (24 hour backup after power failure)
OPERATING SYSTEM: Niagara N4.9u1 or later
POWER: 24 VAC, 50/60 Hz @ 50 VA max; 8 VA nominal
DIMENSIONS: 8.5" x 4.79" x 2.1" (216 x 121.6 x 53 mm); Weight: 1.256 lbs (570 g)
ENCLOSURE: IP20, NEMA 1
MOUNTING: Venturi valve control enclosure, DIN rail (DIN43880, 35mm rail), or surface mount with screws
INSTALLATION GUIDE: 651-320-001

COMMUNICATIONS:
- BACnet MS/TP: RS485, baud rates 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 76800 bps
- BACnet IP: Two RJ45 Ethernet ports; DHCP or static IP
- Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE): Connects to Phoenix Controls Flow Manager Mobile App (Android/iOS)

LED STATUS INDICATORS:
- Small LEDs (green): Communication signal transmission or reception
- Large LEDs: Device status — green (normal), yellow (warning), red (fault)

UNIVERSAL I/Os:
- 16-bit A/D resolution
- UI: 0–10V or 4–20 mA; temperature sensors: 10K NTC Type II/III, 20K NTC, PT100, PT1000, NI1000, PT3000, 100Ω–100kΩ resistive
- AO: Voltage output 0–11V or 4–20mA current
- DO: Dry contact binary output
- SSR: 24VAC/VDC max, 1.5A constant, 3.5A inrush
- Relays: 3 contacts (NO, NC, Common); 10A constant; up to 277VAC; 4 relays; total 12A if commons jumpered

ENVIRONMENTAL:
- Operating: 32–122°F (0–50°C); 5–95% RH non-condensing
- Storage: -40–150°F (-40–66°C)
- Altitude: to 6562 ft (2000 m)

REGULATORY: CE, FCC, ICES, UL/cUL, RoHS3, REACH, Prop 65

APPLICATIONS:
- Research labs: Zone balance, offset control, 1-second response for fume hood containment
- Life Sciences/Vivariums: Zone balance, comfort, humidity, air change diversity
- High-Purity Manufacturing: Precision pressure control, ISO class monitoring
- Healthcare (OR, ICU, pharmacy): Offset control, temperature, occupancy, pandemic modes

content:
FUME HOOD DISPLAY 500 SERIES (FHD500) — MKT-0510 Rev.A 02/2024

OVERVIEW:
Color touch-screen display for fume hoods, used with Phoenix Controls Venturi valves, sash sensors, and Zone Presence Sensors. Works with ACM as part of CSCP. Supports CV, two-position, drive, and VAV applications.

POWER: 24 VAC (±10%), 50/60Hz, Class 2 Transformer ONLY; 360mA max (8.68 VA) @ 24VAC

COMMUNICATIONS: BACnet MS/TP baud rates: 9,600 / 19,200 / 38,400 / 76,800 / 115,000 (Auto-Discovery)

DISPLAY: 24 BPP TFT CTP, 480x480 pixel, 4" diagonal, dimmable LCD; IP54 rated

MONITOR I/Os:
- UI1, UI2, UI01, UI02: Universal inputs — voltage 0-10V ±5%, or dry contact (<100Ω closed, >100kΩ open)
- DO Switch: Relay output, 1 Amp max @ 24VAC

TERMINAL LAYOUT:
- 24VAC Power: HOT and GND terminals
- UI01: Universal I/O 1, GND
- UI02: Universal I/O 2, GND
- UI1: Universal Input 3, GND
- UI2: Universal Input 4, GND
- DO Switch: Generic programmable digital output
- RS485+: BACnet MS/TP positive
- RS485-: BACnet MS/TP negative
Wire gauge: 18–22 AWG (0.5–0.75 mm); solid core recommended

WIRING INSTALLATION (651-000-014):
- Raco 8670, Raco 8650, Raco 8663 or equivalent electrical boxes
- US: 3-1/4" (82.75mm) mounting hole spacing; European: 2-3/8" (60.3mm)
- Mount base plate first, pull wires through hole, terminate with supplied release tool or paper clip
- Mount screen bottom-up at slight angle, align socket/clip holes, press upper corners to snap in, tighten bottom locking screw to 2 in-lbs (0.2 Nm) with 5/64" allen key

RETROFIT MOUNTING OPTIONS:
- RD1: Covers FHD1xx recess mounting holes
- RM1: Covers FHMx00 and FHMx10 recess mounting holes
- RM3: Covers FHMx30 recess mounting holes

SCREEN DISPLAYS:
Normal: Normal operation, setback, flow override, face velocity override, remote exhaust override
Hibernation: Reduced energy mode; shows "OPEN SASH TO RESUME OPERATION"
Alarm/Emergency: Exhaust override, shows emergency messages with exhaust cancel button
Unlinked: FHD500 not connected to ACM — no information displayed
Fail-Safe: Shows "HOOD IN FAIL-SAFE" message

DISPLAY MODES:
- Icon mode: Sash position icon (approximates <1%, 1-33%, 34-66%, 67-99%, >99% open)
- Text only mode: Hood status text in center
- Gauge mode: Face velocity gauge display
- Kiosk: Alternates between sash position and alarms at 2-second intervals

ENERGY FEATURES:
- Hood energy waste indicator: Alerts when sash open + room unoccupied
- Night energy waste alert: Room lights off, instructs user to lower sash
- Hood hibernation: Reduces flow to minimum when no safety concerns

ORDERING GUIDE MODEL STRUCTURE:
FHD 500 — [Network Protocol] — [Options]
- FHD = Fume Hood Display
- 500 = BACnet MS/TP with BLE
- DHV = DHV Interface card in enclosure (retrofit without HUB)
- HUB = Fume Hood HUB: enclosure + interconnect PCB for sash sensors, ZPS, FHD, ACM (retrofit, no DHV)
- HUV = Fume Hood HUB with DHV included

TROUBLESHOOTING:
- Screen black: Check 24VAC at power terminals, check power wire seating
- Communication not working: Check RS485 polarity, EOL 120Ω resistors at both trunk ends, baud rate match, no conflicting MAC/Device ID
- Audible alarm not working: Check volume >0%, check if alarm muted (bell with circle-slash shown)

ENVIRONMENTAL: 32–122°F (0–50°C) operating; -40–150°F storage; 10–90% RH non-condensing
REGULATORY: CE, FCC, ICES, UL/cUL, RoHS3, REACH, Prop 65
